1
    IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026 
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.2237 of 2026)
OM PRAKASH                                         Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF U.P. & ANR.                         
R1 : The State of U.P., Through Principal Secretary
R2 : Sachin
  Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
2. Delay condoned.
3. Leave granted.
4. The present appeal has been filed against the order dated
03.07.2025  passed  in  Criminal  Misc.  Anticipatory  Bail
Cancellation Application No. 138 of 2024 by the High Court of
Judicature at Allahabad 1, in connection with Case Crime No.237
1  Hereinafter referred to as “the High Court”

--- Page 22 ---
2
of  2019  registered  at  Police  Station  Nawabganj,  District
Prayagraj,  Uttar  Pradesh,  for  offences  punishable  under
Sections 504 and 304 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860. 
5. Vide order  dated  28.08.2023  passed  in  Criminal  Misc.
Anticipatory Bail Application No. 9719 of 2023, Respondent No.
2 - accused was granted anticipatory bail. Aggrieved thereby,
the appellant – complainant preferred the aforesaid Civil Misc.
Anticipatory Bail Cancellation Application No. 138 of 2024,
which came to be dismissed by the High Court by the impugned
order.   
6. Earlier, the grievance of the appellant, who is the son of
the deceased, was that the deceased had specifically named
three persons, including Respondent No. 2, as his assailants,
and had subsequently succumbed to the burn injuries sustained
after being set ablaze. However, the said aspect was not duly
considered by the police authorities, who went to the extent of
giving the accused persons a clean chit. In such circumstances,
this Court had directed the Investigating Officer to conduct an
inquiry with regard to the video clip containing the statement
of the deceased.

--- Page 23 ---
3
7. Pursuant thereto, an affidavit was filed by Mr. Kuldeep
Singh Gunawat, Deputy Commissioner of Police, Prayagraj, and
thereafter, by Mr. Jogendra Kumar, Commissioner of Police,
Prayagraj, indicating that the video clip furnished by the
appellant had been sent for forensic examination and was found
to be genuine by the FSL. It was further stated that in view of
such lapse, the Investigating Officer of the case, an officer
of the rank of Inspector presently posted at Aligarh, had been
placed under suspension and departmental proceedings had been
initiated against him.
8. Learned counsel for the appellant submitted that initially,
the police had given a clean chit to three accused persons
including Respondent No.2. However, during the course of trial,
their names surfaced in the evidence, whereupon the trial Court
issued summons to them under Section 319 of the Code of
Criminal Procedure, 19732. It was submitted that thereafter, the
said accused persons, including Respondent No.2 approached the
High Court and secured anticipatory bail, which prompted the
appellant to move the High Court for cancellation of the
anticipatory  bail  granted  to  Respondent  No.  2,  which  was
dismissed in limini. 
2  For short, “Cr.P.C.”

--- Page 24 ---
4
9. Learned counsel for the appellant further submitted that
the  order  granting  anticipatory  bail  had  earlier  been
challenged by the appellant in SLP(Crl.) No. 12157 of 2023
before this Court, but the same was dismissed as withdrawn on
19.11.2024.  Thereafter,  the  accused  persons,  including
Respondent No. 2 allegedly threatened the appellant, leading to
the  registration  of  another  substantive  FIR,  which  was
subsequently  quashed  by  the  High  Court,  against  which
proceedings are proposed to be initiated before this Court. It
was submitted that in view of the subsequent conduct of the
accused,  the  impugned  order  granting  anticipatory  bail  to
Respondent No. 2 is liable to be set aside by this Court.
10. Mr. K.M. Natrajan, learned ASG appearing for the State of
Uttar Pradesh submitted that the authorities have treated the
matter  with  utmost  seriousness  and  that  the  departmental
proceedings initiated against the then Investigating Officer,
would  be  carried  to  their  logical  conclusion  strictly  in
accordance with law, without showing any indulgence. He further
submitted that the FSL report, which had since been received,
has been placed before the concerned trial Court.
11. Learned ASG further submitted that in the light of the

--- Page 25 ---
5
material presently available on record indicating the active
and overt role of the accused persons, including Respondent
No.2, the order granting anticipatory bail deserves to be
interfered with.
12. Per contra, learned senior counsel appearing for Respondent
No.2 submitted that since the grant of anticipatory bail, there
had been no violation of the terms and conditions imposed, and
that the FIR alleging such violation had already been quashed
by the High Court.
13. We have considered the submissions advanced on behalf of
the parties and carefully examined the material placed on
record. It is apparent that, at the relevant point of time when
the police authorities had given Respondent No. 2 a clean chit
and the trial Court had subsequently issued summons under
Section 319 Cr.P.C. during the course of trial, the factual
position stood on a different footing. However, the position
which now emerges discloses the existence of prima facie and
circumstantial evidence of a serious nature, including cogent
documentary material against the accused persons, including
Respondent No. 2. In such circumstances, the continuance of
anticipatory  bail  granted  to  Respondent  No.  2  warrants
interference by this Court.

--- Page 26 ---
6
14. For the reasons aforesaid, the appeal is allowed. The
anticipatory bail granted to Respondent No. 2 is set aside.
Respondent No. 2 is directed to surrender before the court
concerned within a period of two weeks from today. The trial
shall proceed upon submission of the further investigation
report by the police in accordance with law.
15. The Commissioner of Police, Prayagraj is directed to ensure
that further investigation is completed expeditiously and that
the prosecution remains vigilant to ensure that the witnesses
are not influenced by any extraneous considerations and are
duly produced on the dates fixed by the trial Court.
16. Before parting, we clarify that we have expressed no
opinion on the merits of the case. Any observations made herein
are confined solely to the question whether Respondent No. 2
deserves to continue on the anticipatory bail granted by the
High Court vide the impugned order. It is further clarified
that  the  present  appeal  pertains  to  the  cancellation  of
anticipatory bail granted to Respondent No. 2 and not to the
cancellation of regular bail.

--- Page 27 ---
7
17. The criminal appeal stands allowed in the aforementioned
terms.
18. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
          
                                        .......................J.
(AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH)
                          .......................J.
(R. MAHADEVAN)
NEW DELHI
MAY 15, 2026.

--- Page 28 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION NO.  2210 OF 2025  
WITH 
INTERLOCUTORY APPLICATION NO.  115941 OF 2026  
SONU BISHT                                PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
LALIT SINGH BISHT                         RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1.     Heard learned counsel for the petitioner and  
learned counsel for the parties.
2. The parties have settled their dispute through  
mediation conducted by the Supreme Court Mediation  
Centre. The parties have moved an application under  
Article 142 of the Constitution of India for direction  
and  seeking  dissolution  of  marriage  by  decree  of  
divorce.  The  settlement  agreement  recorded  by  the  
Supreme Court Mediation Centre is duly signed by the  
parties, their counsel and the Mediator. The following  
are  the  terms  of  the  settlement  as  mentioned  in  
paragraph Nos.4 to 13 of the settlement agreement dated  
21.02.2026.

--- Page 31 ---
2
“4. WHEREAS after discussions and negotiations  
held between the parties during the mediation  
proceedings,  the  parties  with  the  able  
assistance of their respective Counsels have  
arrived at an amicable settlement wherein,  
they have agreed to settle all their disputes  
on the following terms: -
MARRIAGE AND MAINTENANCE
1. The Parties agree that the marriage  
between them has irretrievably broken down 
due to irreconcilable differences and that 
it shall not be possible for them to  
cohabit together as husband and wife.
2. The Parties agree that they shall  
file  an  appropriate  joint  petition/  
application  under  Article  142  of  the  
Constitution of India before the Hon'ble  
Supreme Court within four weeks from the  
signing  of  the  present  Settlement  
Agreement,  seeking  disposal/quashing  of 
above  mentioned  pending  cases  and  
dissolution of marriage by a decree of  
divorce by mutual consent.
3. The second party agrees to pay a  
lump-sum  of  Rs.9,00,000/-(Rupees  Nine  
Lakhs Only) to the first party as full and 
final settlement of all her claims past,  
present and future and towards all her  
claims arising from/out of such marriage  
including but not limited to interim and  
permanent alimony, stridhan, interim and  
permanent  maintenance  etc.  and  also  
towards the maintenance of the minor child 
Pranika/Naina. The settlement amount of  
Rs.9,00,000/-  (Rupees  Nine  Lakhs  Only) 

--- Page 32 ---
3
shall be paid by the Second Party to the  
First Party by way of demand draft at the  
time  of  appearance  of  parties  and  
consideration  of  the  Petition  Under  
Article  142  of  Constitution  of  India  
before the Hon'ble Supreme Court.
4. The parties have agreed that besides  
the above stated amount of Rs.9,00,000/-  
(Nine Lakhs Only), the Respondent-husband  
shall also return the jewellery articles  
comprising of One pair of gold pauchi  
(kangan), One gold maang tikka, One pair  
of gold earrings, One gold Gents chain,  
Two  simple  Ladies  rings,  One  alphabet 
Gents ring and Two pairs of silver toe  
rings, to the Petitioner/wife towards full 
and final settlement of all her claims  
against jewellery.
5. Petitioner/wife agrees that on receipt  
of the above amount and the jewellery  
stated in the foregoing paras, she shall  
be left with no claim of any kind for  
herself and maintenance of the daughter  
against the Respondent/Husband.
CUSTODY AND VISITATION RIGHTS
6.  It  is  further  agreed  between  the  
parties that the Petitioner shall have the 
custody of the minor girl child i.e.,  
Pranika Bisht alias Naina Bisht. However,  
the Respondent and his mother Smt. Sushma  
Bisht shall be entitled to exercise their  
visitation rights twice every month on a  
Sunday at any public place like park, zoo, 
shopping mall, cinema hall etc. between 10 
am to 5 pm. In the presence of the First  
Party. Whenever the Respondent and his  

--- Page 33 ---
4
mother  Smt.  Sushma  Bisht  intend  to  
exercise their visitation rights, a prior  
intimation to that effect would be sent to 
the Petitioner telephonically or through  
electronic means. The Respondent and his  
mother Smt. Sushma Bisht shall also be  
entitled to interact with the child by  
video  call  after  6  pm  as  per  the  
convenience/daily schedule of the child.  
It is also agreed between the parties that 
the Petitioner and his mother Smt. Sushma  
Bisht shall be entitled to meet the child  
a day prior or a day after her birthday.  
All the expenses for such meetings with  
the child shall be borne by the second  
party. Furthermore, the Respondent and his 
mother are fully entitled to make any gift 
to the daughter as they may deem fit and  
the Petitioner shall have "No Objection"  
thereto. Similarly the Respondent shall be 
at liberty to start any fixed deposit  
account or any other investment for the  
better future of the child. The Respondent 
agrees to share the details of such FD's/  
Investments that may be made by him with  
the Petitioner. The First Party agrees to  
cooperate  and  doing  the  same  wherever 
required.
5. That the parties further agree that they  
shall  also  pray  for  unconditional  
withdrawal/disposal/quashing of the connected  
matters detailed above in accordance with law.
6. It is further agreed between the parties  
that the cases if any, which may not find  
mention in the present Settlement Agreement  
relating to their matrimonial dispute, at the  
instance of the party who had initiated, shall  

--- Page 34 ---
5
be  unconditionally  withdrawn/disposed  of/  
quashed  by  them  in  terms  of  the  instant  
Settlement Agreement.
7. It  is  agreed  between  both  the  parties 
amicably and jointly that the petitioner/ wife  
will have the permanent custody of the minor  
girl  namely  Naina/Pranika  born  from  the  
wedlock  of  the  parties  and  the  mother/  
petitioner has agreed willingly to take up the  
entire responsibility of upbringing, education  
and maintenance (of whatsoever kind) of the  
minor girl namely Naina/Pranika.
8. It is agreed between the parties that they  
will  strictly  adhere  to  the  terms  and  
conditions of this settlement agreement and  
will do all acts and sign all the relevant  
documents, cast personal appearance before the  
Hon'ble  Court  whenever  required  for  the  
purpose of dissolution of their marriage by  
mutual consent/disposal of the pending cases.
9. Both  the  parties  have  executed  this  
settlement agreement before the Ld. Mediator  
appointed by Supreme Court Mediation Centre,  
of their own accord and free will, without any  
force, pressure, undue influence or coercion.  
Both the parties have clearly understood that  
they shall be estopped in law to assail the  
validity of any clause/terms of the present  
settlement agreement on the ground of being  
void and unlawful. Further the parties have  
confirmed and undertaken that they were given  
sufficient time and opportunity to consult  
their legal counsels and it is only after said  
consultations and full understanding of the  
effect of the terms and conditions of the  
present settlement agreement, the parties have  

--- Page 35 ---
6
executed the present settlement agreement. The  
decision  taken  by  them  is  an  informed  
decision.
10. That both the parties have agreed that  
none of them will resort to any kind of act to  
defame/malign the other party in person or  
their respective parents or relatives through  
any media, social media or any forum in any  
other manner whatsoever.
11. Both the parties agree and undertake that  
they  have  settled  all  their  disputes  and  
grievances  against  each  other  and  against  
their family members amicably and they also  
undertake  not  to  file  any  complaints,  
proceedings whether criminal or civil, if any  
against each other or their family members or  
stake any claim in the movable or immovable,  
joint  or  ancestral,  self-acquired  or  HUF  
properties  of  either  parties  and  their  
respective families.
12. It is further agreed between the parties  
and after the dissolution of marriage in terms  
of stated above neither of them shall be left  
with any claim of any nature against each  
other or their family members in future. The  
parties  agree  to  lead  their  lives  
independently and not to interfere in each  
other lives.
13. It is further agreed and understood by the  
parties that in the event of breach of the  
terms of present settlement by any party, the  
party  at  default  shall  be  liable  to  the  
consequences  for  committing  breach  of  the  
terms  as  provided  under  law  including  
committing Contempt of the Hon'ble Court.”

--- Page 36 ---
7
3. In view of above, the marriage between the parties  
solemnized on 27.11.2020 is dissolved by a decree of  
divorce  by  mutual  consent  and  all  pending  
proceedings/cases as mentioned below are quashed.
b)  Petition  under  Section  12  of  Domestic 
Violence  Act,  bearing  Criminal  Misc.  Case 
No.120/2024 tiled “Sonu Singh Bisht vs. Lalit  
Singh  Bisht”,  pending  before  the  District 
Court  Almora,  Uttarakhand  (CNR  
No.UKAL020007502024. 
c) Criminal Misc. Case No.94 of 2024 under  
Section 144 of BNSS, titled “ Sonu Bisht vs.  
Lalit Singh Bisht, pending before the Family  
Court  Almora,  Uttarakhand  (CRN  
No.UKAL0C0002542024).
4.  Accordingly,  the  Interlocutory  Application  
No.115941  of  2026  filed  under  Article  142  of  the 
Constitution  of  India  is  allowed  and  the  Transfer 
Petition is disposed of.  
5. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand  
disposed of.

ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

--- Page 45 ---
SLP (CRL) No. 17508 of 2025 1 
 
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
     CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
 
 
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO                          OF 2026 
(Arising out of Special Leave Petition (Criminal) No.17508 of 2025) 
 
 
 
RAHUL SHUKLA             …APPELLANT 
 
[ 
 
 VERSUS 
 
 
U.T. CHANDIGARH & ANR.                                  …RESPONDENTS 
 
 
 
 
O R D E R 
 
 
Leave granted. 
2. The present appeal arises out of the judgment and final order dated 
18.08.2025 passed by the High Court of Punjab and Haryana at 
Chandigarh in CRM-M No. 60388 of 2024 whereby the High Court partly 
allowed the petition preferred under Section 482 Cr.P.C. and quashed FIR 
No. 47 dated 23.04.2022 registered under Sections 406 and 498 -A IPC at 
Women Police Station, Sector -17, Chandigarh only qua the co -accused 
family members of the appellant, but declined to quash the proceedings 

--- Page 46 ---
SLP (CRL) No. 17508 of 2025 2 
with respect to the present appellant. 
3. The appellant Rahul Shukla, an Australian citizen, was married to 
Neha Chalana, daughter of respondent No.2 – Ravinder Singh, on 
13.04.2018 at Chandigarh. After the marriage, the appellant returned to 
Australia on 26.04.2018, whereas Neha Chalana resumed h er 
employment at Doha, Qatar. Subsequently, on 17.05.2018, she joined the 
appellant in Sydney, Australia, where the parties resided together. 
4. Certain matrimonial disputes arose between the parties during their 
stay in Australia, as such, proceedings relating to domestic violence were  
initiated before the competent court at that place. Ultimately, the Federal 
Circuit Court of Australia, vide decree dated 12.09.2019, dissolved the 
marriage on the ground that the same had irretrievably broken down. The 
decree of divorce was admittedly accepted by both parties. 
5. It is also not in dispute that after dissolution of the marriage, both 
parties moved on with their independent lives. The status report placed 
before the High Court itself records that Neha Chalana remarried on 
09.11.2020 and has since settled abroad. 
6. Nearly three years after passing of the decree of divorce, 
respondent No.2, who is the father of the former wife  (Neha), lodged FIR 
No. 47 dated 23.04.2022 under Sections 406 and 498 -A IPC at Women 

--- Page 47 ---
SLP (CRL) No. 17508 of 2025 3 
Police Station, Sector-17, Chandigarh against the appellant and his family 
members alleging cruelty and dowry-related harassment. 
7. The appellant along with the co -accused family members 
approached the High Court seeking quashing of the aforesaid FIR and all 
consequential proceedings arising therefrom. 
8. The High Court, after considering the material on record, quashed 
the FIR qua petitioner Nos.2 to 4 therein, namely the mother and sisters of 
the appellant, observing that the allegations against them were vague and 
omnibus in nature and continuation of pr oceedings against them would 
amount to abuse of process of law. 
9. However, insofar as the present appellant is concerned, the High 
Court declined to exercise jurisdiction under Section 482 Cr.P.C. solely 
on the ground that despite having been granted anticipatory bail vide 
order dated 23.12.2024, the appellant had travel led to Australia in 
January, 2025 without obtaining prior permission from the Court 
concerned. 
10. The relevant observations of the High Court read as under: 
“Petitioner No.1 Rahul Shukla went to Australia in January, 
2025 and that too without taking permission from the Court 
concerned. Therefore, petitioner No.1 has misused the 
concession of anticipatory bail granted in his favour. In 
light of aforesaid factua l position, I do not deem it fit to 

--- Page 48 ---
SLP (CRL) No. 17508 of 2025 4 
quash the aforesaid FIR registered against him and petition 
qua petitioner No.1 Rahul Shukla is accordingly 
dismissed.” 
 
11. Having heard learned counsel appearing for the parties and upon 
perusal of the material placed on record, we are of the considered view 
that the approach adopted by the High Court insofar as the present 
appellant is concerned, is unsustainable in law. 
12. The undisputed factual position emerging from the record is that the 
matrimonial disputes between the appellant and Neha Chalana arose 
during their stay in Australia , after the marriage was solemnized on 
13.04.2018. Admittedly, after May 2018, the parties continued to reside 
abroad and the alleged incidents forming subject matter of the FIR are 
stated to have taken place in Australia. 
13. It is also undisputed that the marriage between the parties stood 
dissolved by a decree dated 12.09.2019 passed by the Federal Circuit 
Court of Australia and the said decree has attained finality. The former 
wife has also remarried and settled abroad. 
14. Despite the aforesaid admitted position, the FIR in question came to 
be lodged only on 23.04.2022 by the father of the former wife after 
considerable delay and long after the parties had severed their 
matrimonial ties and moved on with their respective lives. 

--- Page 49 ---
SLP (CRL) No. 17508 of 2025 5 
15. Significantly, the High Court itself recorded findings that the 
allegations against the family members of the appellant were vague and 
unsustainable and that continuation of criminal proceedings against them 
would amount to misuse of the process of law. On ce such findings had 
been returned on the same factual foundation, there was no justifiable 
reason to deny similar relief to the present appellant solely on account of 
his travelling abroad. 
16. The reason assigned by the High Court, namely that the appellant 
had travelled to Australia without obtaining prior permission from the 
Court, could at best constitute an issue relating to compliance of bail 
conditions, which were not contrary to his action . The same, by itself, 
could not furnish a valid ground to continue criminal proceedings which 
otherwise, in the peculiar facts and circumstances of the present case, ex 
facie not only appear to be not maintainable but also manifestly afflicted 
with mala fides. 
17. The appellant is admittedly an Australian citizen residing and 
employed in Australia. There is nothing on record to indicate that he 
intended to evade the process of law permanently.  
18. It is also relevant to note that the complainant herein is not the 
former wife herself, but her father. There is no material on record to 

--- Page 50 ---
SLP (CRL) No. 17508 of 2025 6 
suggest that the former wife was either incapacitated or prevented from 
availing remedies available to her in law, if so advised. 
19. In the totality of the circumstances, particularly having regard to the 
fact that the marriage stood dissolved in the year 2019; that both parties 
accepted the decree of divorce and moved on in their respective lives; 
that the former wife remarried in the year 2020; and that the FIR came to 
be lodged thereafter by the father of the former wife after considerable 
delay, we are satisfied that continuation of the criminal prosecution 
against the appellant would amount to abuse of the process of law and 
would not serve the ends of justice. 
20. Consequently, the impugned judgment and order dated 18.08.2025 
passed by the High Court of Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh in CRM -
M No. 60388 of 2024, insofar as it declines relief to the appellant Rahul 
Shukla, is set aside. 
21. FIR No. 47 dated 23.04.2022 registered under Sections 406 and     
498-A IPC at Women Police Station, Sector -17, Chandigarh and all 
consequential proceedings arising therefrom qua the appellant Rahul 
Shukla stand quashed. 
 
22. The appeal is accordingly allowed. 

--- Page 51 ---
SLP (CRL) No. 17508 of 2025 7 
23. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
 
 
 
 
…………………………………………..J. 
(SANJAY KAROL) 
 
 
 
 
……….…………………………………..J. 
(NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH) 
 
April 15, 2026 
New Delhi  

--- Page 52 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NOS.8091-8093   OF 2026
(Arising out of S.L.P.(CIVIL) NOS.7744-7746 of 2026)
THE SPECIAL CHIEF SECRETARY & ORS.   ...Appellant(s)
                  Vs.
K V MOHAN RAO & ORS.   ...Respondent(s)
         
        
 O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. A 40 year battle hopefully will come to an end with
this order.  The present appeals call in question the
correctness of the judgment dated 8th October, 2025 passed by
the High Court of Andhra Pradesh at Amaravati in Civil
Revision Petition Nos.6302, 6303 and 6306 of 2017.
3. By the impugned order, the High Court confirmed the
order of attachment dated 6 th October, 2017 made by the III
Additional District Judge, Kurnool.
4. The proceedings themselves arise out of an Award in
favour of the respondent No.1 against  M/s. Nandyal Co-
operative Spinning Mills Ltd. The Award dated 27 th February,
1999 was for a sum of Rs. 32,62,779/- (Rupees Thirty Two
Lakh Sixty Two Thousand Seven Hundred and Sevety Nine Only).
1

--- Page 53 ---
5. The respondent No. 1 filed Execution Petition No. 2
of 2004 before the III Additional District Judge seeking to
execute the Award. Thereafter, the respondent No.1 on the
ground that the Government held substantial shareholding in
M/s. Nandyal Co-operative Spinning Mills Ltd. sought to
execute the decree against the properties of the Government.
Consequential orders of attachments were made and notices to
bring the property to sale of the Government were issued.
The High Court has confirmed the orders directing execution
against the properties of the Government.
6. When the matter came up for hearing on 13 th March,
2026,  Mr. N. Venkataraman, learned Additional Solicitor
General  assisted  by  Ms.  Prerna  Singh,  learned  counsel
appearing for the State Submitted that properties of the
State could not have been proceeded with, since Government
is different from its instrumentality.
7. On the other hand, Mr. DVSS Somayajulu, learned
Senior Counsel for the respondent No.1 contended that in
this case more than 98 per cent of the shares of judgment
award  debtor is  held  by  the  Government  and  that  the
Government in certain other aspects have granted relief by
granting amounts to satisfy other debts.
2

--- Page 54 ---
8. Before  examining  the  controversy  and  without
prejudice to the  rights and contentions of the respective
parties, we made the following order on 13th March, 2026:
“1. Having heard Mr.  N. Venkataraman , learned
Additional Solicitor General of India assisted by
Ms. Prerna Singh, learned advocate appearing for the
petitioners and Mr. DVSS Somayajulu, learned Senior
Advocate appearing for respondent No.1, we are of
the opinion that before we hear further, a meeting
which should be summoned by the Chief Secretary,
State of Andhra Pradesh between respondent No.1 -
Contractor and the liquidator of M/s. Nandyal Co-
operative Spinning Mills Ltd. to explore an amicable
resolution to the dispute. We say so for the reason
that  even  though  the  award  is  of  27.02.1999,
respondent No.1 has not received any amount and
today execution proceedings are going on. We are in
the year 2026. This reflects a sad commentary. In
any  event  without  prejudice  to  the  rights  and
contentions of the petitioners and respondents, let
a meeting be summoned and an attempt be made at an
amicable resolution of the dispute. We direct the
State  to  play  an  active  role  to  resolve  this
dispute. 
2.Mr.  DVSS  Somayajulu,  learned  Senior  Advocate
appearing  for  respondent  No.1  submits  that  no
precipitative  action  pursuant  to  the  attachment
would be taken. 
3.List on 11.05.2026.”
9. Thereafter, meetings were held pursuant to our order
presided over by the Chief Secretary of the State. The
situation today is that without prejudice to the rights and
contentions and considering that this was an award of 1999,
the State, as a one-time measure and without it being
3

--- Page 55 ---
treated as a precedent, offers a sum of Rs. 2,55,14,874/-
(Rupees Two Crore Fifty Five Lakh Fourteen Thousand Eight
Hundred and Seventy Four Only) in full and final settlement.
The matter was further adjourned for the respondents to
consider the offer.
10. The  respondent  No.1  has  today  filed  an
application setting out the calculation as made by him.
According to the respondent No.1, what is due is the sum of
Rs.5,64,48,067.41/- (Rupees Five Crore Sixty Four Lakh Forty
Eight Thousand and Sixty Seven Only).
11. Considering the overall facts and circumstances, we
suggested that the matter should be closed by the appellants
by offering a consolidated sum of Rs.3,00,00,000/- (Rupees
three crore) in full and final settlement.
12. Parties ultimately left the matter to the Court.
13. Keeping the overall facts and circumstances, we are
of the opinion that, in the peculiar facts of the case, the
appellants  should  be  directed  to  pay  a  sum  of
Rs.3,00,00,000/- (Rupees three crore) in full and final
settlement.  If such a payment is made that will be in full
and final settlement of the Award dated 27 th February, 1999
inclusive of all interest. We direct the appellants to pay
the amount of Rs.3,00,00,000/- (Rupees three crore) within a
period of eight weeks from today.
4

--- Page 56 ---
14.  Considering the nature of the order made, the
question of law is left open.
15. Our present order will be in supersession of all the
orders of the Courts below.
16. With the result, the orders of the High Court will
stand  set  aside.  The  amount  shall  be  released  by  the
appellants within a period of 8 weeks.
17. The order is made in the special facts of the case
and shall not to be treated as a precedent.
18. The appeals are accordingly disposed of.
..........................J.
       (K.V.VISWANATHAN)
                          
 ..........................J.
       (VIJAY BISHNOI) 
NEW DELHI;
May 21, 2026.
5

--- Page 57 ---

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Leave granted.

CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.      /2026
[Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.6665/2026]
 TAUHID ALAM 
KITABULLAH SHAIKH           APPELLANT
      VERSUS
STATE OF MAHARASHTRA          RESPONDENT
        O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The High Court of Judicature at Bombay, by
the  impugned  judgment  and  order  dated  18 th
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.6665/2026 1

--- Page 60 ---
March,  2026,  has  dismissed  the  appellant’s
prayer for grant of regular bail. 
3. Appellant,  figuring  as  an  accused  in  FIR
No.0396 of 2024 dated 18th May, 2024 registered
at Police Station Shivaji Nagar, District Mumbai,
Maharashtra, is in custody since 18 th May, 2024.
It  is  alleged  in  the  FIR  that  the  appellant
committed offence(s) punishable under Sections
8(c),  22  and  29  of  Narcotic  Drugs  and
Psychotropic Substances Act, 19851.
4. We have heard learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
5. No doubt, 15 sealed bottles of cough syrup
containing  Codeine  Phosphate  were  allegedly
recovered  and  charge-sheet  under  Section
173(2) of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973
has been filed on 22 nd October, 2024; however,
the charges are yet to be framed.
6. Prosecution  proposes  to  examine  11
1 NDPS Act
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.6665/2026 2

--- Page 61 ---
witnesses to drive home the charges against the
appellant. Since no witness evidence has been
recorded to date, the trial is likely to take some
time to conclude.
7. T aking an overall view of the matter, we are
of the considered opinion that further detention
of the appellant pending trial is not necessary;
and, since the appeal deserves acceptance, the
appellant may be admitted to an order for grant
of bail.
8. Accordingly,  we  set  aside  the  impugned
judgment and order.
9. Appellant shall be released on bail, subject
to furnishing of bail bonds to the satisfaction of
the trial court and subject to such other terms
and conditions as may be imposed by it.
10. Needless to observe, the appellant shall not,
directly  or  indirectly,  by  making  inducement,
threat  or  promise,  dissuade  any  person
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.6665/2026 3

--- Page 62 ---
acquainted  with  the  facts  of  the  case  from
disclosing such facts to the court.
11. In  the  event  there  is  any  breach  of  the
terms and conditions for grant of bail, the trial
court shall be at liberty to cancel the bail of the
appellant.
12. It is  also ordered that  the  appellant shall
diligently attend proceedings of the trial, unless
exempted.  If  he  abstains  from  attending  the
proceedings without justifiable cause, that could
also  be  seen  as  breach  of  the  conditions  for
grant of bail and the trial court will be free to
pass appropriate orders.
13. We  clarify  that  the  observations  made  in
this order and grant of bail will not be treated as
findings on the merits of the case.
14. The appeal is, accordingly, allowed on the
aforesaid terms.
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.6665/2026 4

disposed of.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard learned senior counsel appearing for the parties.
2. Issue notice.
3. There shall be an order of status quo and we make it explicitly
clear that the Hundis that are placed inside the temple or within
2

--- Page 68 ---
the premises of the temple, shall not be opened by any of the
petitioners. However, in the presence of the petitioners and
respondents’ officials, the nominated officials of the Indian Bank
shall open the Hundis, if necessary, and deposit the amounts found
in the Hundi to the account of the temple at Indian Bank, which
shall not be allowed to be withdrawn by any person.
4. Mr. Jaideep Gupta, learned senior counsel appearing for the
respondent/State submits, on instructions, that the directions
issued in paragraph 24 (iii) of the impugned order has already been
complied with. His submission is placed on record.
5. Counter affidavit, if any, shall be filed within six weeks and
rejoinder affidavit, within three weeks thereafter.
6. List after nine weeks.
7. In view of the above directions, proceedings pursuant to notice
dated 24.04.2026 issued by the Commissioner of Hindu Religious and
Charitable Endowments Department filed in IA No.141874/2026 as
Annexure A-6 stands stayed.

         IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.             /2026
[Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.8393/2026]
AUSHOTOSH PARATE                 APPELLANT
VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH            RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The High Court of Chhattisgarh at Bilaspur, by a
judgment  and  order  dated  22 nd April,  2025,  has
rejected  the  prayer  of  the  appellant for  bail  in
anticipation of arrest. This appeal is directed against
such judgment and order.
3. Appellant figures as an accused in FIR No.0767
of 2024 dated 24 th September, 2024 registered at
Police  Station  Khamtarai,  District  Raipur,
Chhattisgarh  under  Sections  408,  420,  467,  468,
469,  470,  471  and  34  of  the  Indian  Penal  Code,
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.8393/2026 1

--- Page 79 ---
1860. 
4. We have heard learned counsel appearing for
the parties.
5. Learned counsel appearing for the respondent
submits that the appellant has joined investigation
and  cooperated  with  the  investigating  officer  in
terms of the earlier orders of this Court.
6. It also appears that chargesheet has been filed
on 25th December, 2024 but the charges are yet to
be  framed;  presently,  argument  on  charges  is  in
progress.  Prosecution  proposes  to  examine  11
(eleven)  witnesses  to  drive  home  the  charges
against the appellant. Thus, the trial is likely to take
some time to conclude.
7. Also,  co-accused,  i.e.,  Deepak  Sah  has  been
enlarged  on  bail  and  the  appellant  is  entitled  to
claim parity.
8. T aking an overall view of the matter, we are of
the considered opinion that the appellant’s detention
for custodial interrogation is not necessary and that
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.8393/2026 2

--- Page 80 ---
he may be admitted to an order for grant of bail in
anticipation of arrest.
9. Accordingly,  we  set  aside  the  impugned
judgment and order.
10. It is directed that in the event of the appellant
being arrested, he shall be released on bail on terms
and conditions to be imposed by the trial court.
11. Since the investigation qua the appellant is yet
to  conclude,  we  direct  that  if  the  investigating
officer  calls  upon  the  appellant  to  join  the
investigation, he shall do so by attending the police
station  failing which liberty is granted to bring any
such omission to the notice of the trial court and
seek cancellation of bail.
12. Needless  to  observe,  the  appellant shall not,
directly or indirectly, by making inducement, threat
or  promise,  dissuade  any  person  acquainted  with
the facts of the case from disclosing such facts to
any police officer or to the court.
13. We clarify that the observations made in this
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.8393/2026 3

--- Page 81 ---
order  and  grant  of  bail  to  the  appellant  in
anticipation of arrest will not be treated as findings
on the merits of the case.
14. The  appeal  is,  accordingly,  allowed  on  the
aforesaid terms.
15. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed
of.
............................................J.
(DIPANKAR DATTA)
............................................J.
(SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA)
New Delhi;
May 22, 2026.

--- Page 96 ---
1
    IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026 
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.18167 of 2025)
DARWIN BENAIAH ANOSE                               Appellant(s)
                             VERSUS
SASIKALA & ORS.    
R1 : Sasikala
R2 : T. Karthickraja
R3 : The Inspector of Police, Manimangalam Police Station,
Manimangalam, Chennai, Tamil Nadu
                                 Respondent(s)
WITH 
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026 
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.2801 of 2026)
DARWIN BENAIAH ANOSE                               Appellant(s)
                              VERSUS
THIRUGNANAM & ANR.    
           
R1 : Thirugnanam 
R2 : The State represented by The Inspector of Police, 
Manimangalam Police Station, Kanchipuram District, Tamil 
Nadu
                                 Respondent(s)
                
O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
2. Leave granted.
3. The present appeals have been filed seeking cancellation of

--- Page 97 ---
2
anticipatory bail granted to the private respondents who are
the relatives of the husband of the deceased. 
4. The allegation against the husband and his relatives was
that they were party to the brutal assault on the deceased
leading to her death. 
5. Learned  counsel  for  the  appellant  submitted  that  the
private respondents, being present in the house where this
assault took place, cannot feign either ignorance or non-
involvement for the reason that being in the house and being
aware that a person is being brutally assaulted, that too the
daughter-in-law  of  the  house  cannot  be  accepted.  It  was
submitted that the postmortem report also reveals the brutal
attack as there were multiple injuries, including breaking of
the rib of the deceased. It was contended that even after the
assault, the deceased was left to die and no medical assistance
was provided to her. It was submitted that if the private
respondents take a stand that they had no role, then at least
at that point of time when the husband of the deceased had
stopped assaulting her, it was their duty to take the deceased
to the nearby medical facility and that not being done clearly
indicates that with common intention, the deceased was brutally
beaten to death. 
6. Learned  counsel  submitted  that  it  was  the  informant/
complainant, being the brother of the deceased, who having
reached the matrimonial home upon information, had taken the

--- Page 98 ---
3
deceased to the hospital where she was declared dead. 
7. Learned counsel for the State submitted that the reports
indicate that all the private respondents were present in the
house and clearly their connivance and partnership in the crime
cannot be ruled out. 
8. Learned counsel for the father-in-law and brother-in-law,
submitted that this is a case for cancellation of bail and none
of the conditions thereof are satisfied. It was submitted that
the said accused have not misused their privilege of bail. It
was further contended that they are ready to abide by any
stringent condition which the Court may direct for. 
9. Nobody has appeared on behalf of the remaining private
respondents, despite valid service of notice. 
10. Having considered the matter in its entirety, we find that
the High Court vide the impugned order has erroneously granted
bail  to  the  private  respondents.  There  being  sufficient
material to indicate their presence and the manner in which
they conducted themselves, both during the assault and even
thereafter,  does  lead  to  strong  indication  of  their
involvement. Moreover, at the time of considering grant of
anticipatory  bail/bail,  the  Court  has  only  to  go  by  the
probabilities and the circumstances and not delve into a deep
consideration of either the prosecution or the defence version.
In the present case, the aforesaid facts not being disputed and
the postmortem report indicating brutal assault, should have

--- Page 99 ---
4
been sufficient for the Court not to interfere. Instead, it
should have been left for the Trial Court to consider the
matter where there would have been materials to go into such
preliminary issues more in depth. 
11. For the reasons aforesaid, the impugned orders are set
aside  and  the  anticipatory  bail  granted  to  the  private
respondents stand cancelled. They are directed to appear before
the Court below latest within two weeks from today. 
12. Consequently, the appeals stand allowed.
13. It goes without saying that the observations made in the
present order are only for the purposes of considering whether
the High Court had rightly exercised its jurisdiction while
granting anticipatory bail to the private respondents, in the
peculiar facts and circumstances of this case and thus would
not prejudice the case of either side during the trial.
14. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
          
                                        .......................J.
        (AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH)
                          .......................J.
(R. MAHADEVAN)
NEW DELHI
MAY 18, 2026.

--- Page 100 ---

O R D E R
1. The present Transfer Petitions have been filed for
the transfer of the following proceedings:
“1. Petition No.B-136 of 2025, titled
“Vivek  Viswanathan  vs.  Neelam  Pandey”,
pending before the Family Court, Bandra,
Mumbai  to  the  Family  Court,  Indore,
Madhya Pradesh;
2. Original  Petition  No.1703/2024,
titled  “Vivek  Viswanathan  vs.  Neelam
Pandey”, pending before the Family Court,
Palakkad,  Kerala  to  the  Family  Court,
Indore, Madhya Pradesh; and
1

--- Page 109 ---
3. Original  Petition  No.1749/2024,
titled  “Vivek  Viswanathan  vs.  Neelam
Pandey”, pending before the Family Court,
Palakkad,  Kerala  to  the  Court  Family
Court, Indore, Madhya Pradesh.
2. The grounds raised by the petitioner for transfer are:
she has no source of income; she has to take care of her
minor child; and on account of long distance, she is unable
to effectively defend the proceedings initiated at Bandra
and Palakkad.
3. Having  heard  learned  counsel  for  the  parties  and
considered the submissions advanced at the Bar, we are of
the considered view that the interest of justice would be
best  met  with  the  transfer  of  the  aforementioned
proceedings to the Court having competent jurisdiction at
Patiala  House  Courts,  Tilak  Marg,  New  Delhi.  Ordered
accordingly.
4. Accordingly,  the  present  Transfer  Petitions  are
allowed. 
5. The records shall be forwarded to the transferee Court
as expeditiously as possible.
6. Parties are directed to appear before the concerned
Court on 01.07.2026.
7. The  respondent-husband  is  permitted  to  attend  the
proceedings virtually, unless his physical presence is
required by the concerned Court.
2

--- Page 110 ---
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

ITEM NO.1               COURT NO.17               SECTION II
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  2556/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated  29-10-2025 in
CRMBA No. 37640/2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad]
PREMCHANDRA                                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
ABHISHEK PATHAK & ANR.                             Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION , IA No. 27869/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T., IA
No. 27868/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDL.DOCS./FACTS/ANNEXURES, IA
No. 139763/2026 - RECALL OF ORDER OF NON-BAILABLE WARRANT 
WITH
SLP(Crl) No. 3581/2026 (II)
IA No. 50591/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T., IA No. 50585/2026 -
PERMISSION TO FILE ADDL.DOCS./FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 20-05-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Madhav Bhatia, Adv.
                   Ms. Simran Verma, Adv.
                   Ms. Simran Verama, Adv.
                   Mr. Rohit Amit Sthalekar, AOR
Mr. Ankit Khera, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Praful Jindal, Adv.
                   Mr. Anurag Tandon, AOR                   
                   
                   Mr. Bhakti Vardhan Singh, AOR
                   Mr. Upendra Mishra, Adv.
                   Mr. Narendra Pandey, Adv.
                   Mr. J Tarun Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Ankit Khatri, Adv.
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Madhav Bhatia, learned counsel for the de-facto
complainant, Mr. Bhakti Vardhan Singh, learned counsel for the

--- Page 119 ---
2
State and Mr. Praful Jindal, learned counsel for respondent
no.1 in both the matters respectively.
2. The present Special Leave Petitions call in question the
correctness of the orders dated 29.10.2025 and 31.10.2025 in
Criminal Misc.Bail Application Nos.37640/2025 and 30712/2025
passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Judicature  at  Allahabad
respectively.
3. The  respondents  are  father  and  son  in  these  two
petitions.  Abhishek  Pathak-accused  respondent  no.1  in
SLP(Crl.)No.2556/2026  is  the  son  of  respondent  no.1-Ram
Bahadur alias Ramai Pathak in SLP(Crl) No. 3581/2026. Both the
accused are facing trial in case arising out of FIR No.85/2025
registered with Police Station Kaptanganj, District Basti,
Uttar Pradesh, for the offences punishable under Sections
103(1) & 238 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (for short,
‘BNS’).
4. According to the prosecution, these two respondents-
accused persons are involved in the alleged murder of deceased
Dhruvchandra, the brother of the informant. Abhishek Pathak-
accused was arrested on 06.06.2025 and Ram Bahadur alias Ramai
Pathak was arrested on 02.06.2025.
5. The High Court has passed a very brief order enlarging
the respondents on bail. As far as accused-Abhishek Pathak is
concerned, on the day when the High Court has passed the
order,  he  had  the  following  three  criminal  antecedents
including FIR No.85/2025:-

--- Page 120 ---
3
S.no. FIR/PS Case No. Section Police Station District
1 238/2025 304(2),317(2) BNS Kalwari Basti
2 226/2023 Sections 109, 363, 366, 
366-A, 376AB IPC and 
Sections 17,5m and 6 of the
Protection of Children from
Sexual Offences (POCSO) 
Act, 2012
Kaptanganj Basti
3 194/2022 354, 504, 506 IPC and 
Sections 7,8 of the 
Protection of Children from
Sexual Offences (POCSO) 
Act, 2012
Nagar Basti
4 843/2017 323, 504, 506 IPC Nagar Basti
6. Today, it is undisputed that post the enlargement of bail
accused-Abhishek Pathak is involved in one more antecedent
namely FIR No.238/2025 under Sections 304(2) and 317(2) of the
BNS.
7. On the day when the respondents-accused were enlarged on
bail, they have undergone approximately 05 months in custody.
8. The motive of the accused as alleged by the prosecution
is that the deceased Dhruvchandra was suspected to be having
an affair with the sister of the accused-Abhishek Pathak and
daughter of accused- Ram Bahadur alias Ramai Pathak. Those
issues will be thrashed out at the trial.
9. However, we are satisfied that as far as bail granted to
accused-Abhishek Pathak in SLP(Crl.)No.2556/2026 is concerned,
is deserves to be set aside. The High Court in the impugned
order(s) proceeds on the basis that accused-Abhishek Pathak
had only one criminal antecedent whereas on the day when the
bail  order  was  passed,  there  were  total  three  criminal

--- Page 121 ---
4
antecedents  against  accused-Abhishek  Pathak.  The  offences
concern in the antecedent cases are also heinous in nature.
10. Keeping the overall facts and circumstances of the cases
and applying the test of prima facie case, we are convinced
that the bail granted to accused-Abhishek Pathak deserves to
be set aside.
11. Insofar as accused- Ram Bahadur alias Ramai Pathak is
concerned, admittedly there are no criminal antecedents.
12. Learned counsel for the  de-facto complainant points to
the disclosure statement and the discoveries made thereof.
Those are also has to be thrashed out at the trial.
13. Keeping the overall facts and circumstances, we are not
inclined to interfere with the discretion of grant of bail
exercise insofar as accused-Ram Bahadur alias Ramai Pathak is
concerned in SLP(Crl.)No.3581/2026.
14. For the reasons set out above, while the prayer of the de
facto  complainant is allowed and the impugned order is set
aside in SLP(Crl.)No.2556/2026.
15. Two  weeks’  time  is  granted  to  accused-Abhishek
Pathak(respondent no.1 in SLP(Crl.)No.2556/2026) to surrender
before the concerned District Court Basti, Uttar Pradesh.
16. On  surrender,  the  said  District  Court  shall  remand
accused-Abhishek Pathak to judicial custody.
17. With the above observations, SLP(Crl.)No.3581/2026 will
stand dismissed and SLP(Crl.)No.2556/2026 will stand disposed
of.

--- Page 122 ---
5
18. All pending applications including IA No. 139763/2026
shall also stand disposed of.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (POONAM VAID)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

--- Page 123 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL/CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.154 OF 2025
SWATI JAIN & ORS.   ..... PETITIONERS
              VERSUS
PORUSH JAIN ..... RESPONDENT
WITH
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.953/2025
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.148/2025
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 954/2025 
O R D E R
1.  These  transfer  petitions  filed  by  petitioner-wife  seeking
transfer of the petitions filed by respondent-husband at Gautam
Budh Nagar, Chandigarh, Uttarakhand before the jurisdictional court
at Meerut.
2.These matters were listed on more than 10 occasions before this
Court. Precious judicial time was spent on this. Vide order dated
25.06.2025 the matter was referred to Supreme Court Mediation
Centre since the dispute essentially was between husband and wife
and also related to the custody of the child and visitation rights.
After laborious efforts of the learned mediator, a settlement was
entered into before the mediation centre under which certain sums
of  money  was  to  be  paid  by  the  respondent-husband  to  the
petitioner-wife, pursuant to which, a sum of Rs.2 crores has
1

--- Page 124 ---
already been paid to the petitioner-wife, which was part of the
permanent alimony to both the petitioner-wife and son. 
3. When the matter was listed before the Court for acceptance of
settlement agreement and passing orders on the settlement agreement
entered into between the parties under Section 89 CPC, a pointed
question was posed to both the parties as to whether they are
agreeable to the terms and conditions set out in the agreement and
both parties in chorus submitted that there were certain issues
which have to be ironed out though dispute having been resolved
substantially. Hence, for ironing out the creases, we sought
assistance of a trained mediator and requested Mr. R Basant,
learned senior counsel and member of this Bar, to resolve the same.
4. This court had been informed that learned senior Mediator was in
pains to convince both the parties which have been in vain or in
other words, both the parties did not bend.
5. Be that as it may. At this juncture, learned counsel for the
petitioner, who has filed transfer petitions seeks leave of the
court to withdraw the petitions.
6. Out of the aforesaid Rs.2 crores already paid, pursuant to the
settlement agreement, which has not come through, we deem it
necessary to direct the petitioner-wife to refund Rs.1 crore within
10 weeks from today to the respondent-husband, failing which, the
respondent-husband would be at liberty to proceed to recover the
same by filing appropriate execution petition.
7. Having regard to the fact that both the parties have made this
Court,  learned  arbitrator  and  mediation  centre  to  spend
considerable precious judicial time and even after having entered
2

--- Page 125 ---
into an agreement of settlement not ironing out the creases and
sticking to their stand by not budging for reasons best known,
which would clearly exhibit their recalcitrant attitude. As such,
we permit petitioner to withdraw these petitions and direct both
the parties to pay sum of Rs.1 Lakh each to the Supreme Court
Mediation Centre as costs.
8. Transfer petitions stands dismissed as withdrawn subject to payment
of costs of Rs.1 Lakh each by the parties to the Supreme Court
Mediation Centre.
..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(PRASANNA B. VARALE)
New Delhi;
May 21, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.              OF 2026
(@SLP(CRL.) NO.14078 OF 2025)
SALMA KHATOON ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
STATE OF BIHAR & ORS. ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. Appellant, who has been arraigned as an accused in FIR No.
75/2025 for the offences punishable under Sections 115(2),
117(2), 118, 126(2), 74, 109(1), 352, 351(2) and 3(5) of
Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (BNS), is seeking for being
enlarged on anticipatory bail.
4. This court by order dated 16.10.2025 had granted interim
protection. According to the prosecution the appellant along
with others had formed a large group of persons and they are
1                                                                                       SLP(CRL.) NO.14078 OF 2025

--- Page 134 ---
alleged to have rushed to the spot armed with lathis, farsas,
bhalas and gadasas after Sheikh Zakir called for support.
5. Perusal of the FIR would indicate that Zakir, Teyazzudin
and  Hasantaz  having  been  named  specifically  as  having
inflicted  grievous  injuries  on  Sheikh  Jahangir  and  his
daughter,  in  other  words,  there  are  omnibus  allegations
against the present appellant Salma Khatoon and there is no
overt act alleged against her or weapon having been used by
her. Perusal of the medical report would also indicate that
complainant’s  husband  Sheikh  Jahangir  had  suffered  two
injuries which according to the prosecution are attributable
to the other co-accused.
6. In these circumstances, we are of the considered view that
interim protection granted on 16.10.2025 deserves to be made
absolute.
7. Accordingly, appeal is allowed. Impugned order is set aside
and we direct the Investigating Officer (IO) to release the
appellant on anticipatory bail on such terms and conditions as
he  deems  fit  including  the  condition  of  directing  the
2                                                                                       SLP(CRL.) NO.14078 OF 2025

--- Page 135 ---
appellant to cooperate with the investigation and to appear
before the trial court on all dates of hearing except when
exempted for any specific reason.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(PRASANNA B. VARALE)
New Delhi;
May 20, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.7784/2026]
ONKAR LAL                           Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF RAJASTHAN                Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The appellant stands convicted in Sessions
Case  No.160/2025  (CIS  No.160/2025)  for  the
offences punishable under Sections 302 and 34 of
the Indian Penal Code, 1860. He has been sentenced
to undergo imprisonment for life.  His application
for suspension of sentence was rejected by the
High Court.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
Notwithstanding the fact that the appellant
has  undergone  five  years  of  sentence,  his
application  for  suspension  of  sentence  was
rejected by the High Court on the premise that the
1

--- Page 139 ---
appellant stands on a different footing from that
of the co-accused. We take note of the fact that
all the co-accused have been convicted. In such
view of the matter, the difference drawn by the
High Court cannot be sustained in the eye of law.
Further, the appeal is of the year 2025, which
will take substantial time to be disposed of.  We
also take note of the fact that the appellant has
undergone incarceration for more than 5 years.
In such view of the matter, we are inclined
to set aside the impugned order and suspend the
sentence of the appellant and grant him bail,
during the pendency of his appeal before the High
Court, on terms and conditions to the satisfaction
of the Trial Court.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the sentence of the appellant stands
suspended and he is granted bail, during the
pendency of his appeal before the High Court, on
terms and conditions to the satisfaction of the
concerned Trial Court.
2

--- Page 140 ---
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
..........................J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
..........................J.
[NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH]
NEW DELHI;
MAY 22, 2026.
3

--- Page 141 ---

Leave granted.
The  appellant  seeks  regular  bail  in
connection  with  Complaint  No.  File  No.
DGGI/INT/INTL/755/2025-GR-N,  dated  13.08.2025
filed before the Hon’ble Special Court (Economic
Offences), District-Jaipur Metro-II, Jaipur for
the  offences  punishable  under  Sections
132(1)(a),(f),(g),(h) and (l) of the Central Goods

--- Page 144 ---
2
and Services Tax Act, 2017 (for short, ‘CGST
Act’). 
We have heard the learned Senior counsel
appearing for the appellant and the learned ASG
appearing for the respondent-Union of India.
We have been informed that the appellant has
already undergone incarceration for a period of
more than 9 months. The charge sheet has already
been filed in the present case. 
In such view of the matter, the continued
incarceration  of  the  appellant  is  no  longer
required,  especially,  by  taking  note  of  the
maximum punishment that can be awarded against
the appellant for the offences punishable under
Section 132 of the CGST Act, in the event of the
charges being proved against him.
In such view of the matter, we are inclined
to set aside the impugned order passed by the High
Courtand grant bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the appellant is granted bail on the

--- Page 145 ---
3
terms and conditions subject to the satisfaction
of the concerned Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.

disposed of.

of the year 2025.

Leave granted.
The  appellant  apprehends  his  arrest  in
connection with FIR No.10/2026, dated 22.01.2026,
registered at Police Station Jhanpada, District-
Dausa, for the offence punishable under Section
136 of the Electricity Act, 2003.

--- Page 154 ---
2
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
It is not in dispute that, pursuant to our
order dated 11.05.2026, the appellant has joined
the investigation.
Considering the above, we are inclined to set
aside the impugned order and grant anticipatory
bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the appellant is granted anticipatory
bail on terms and conditions to the satisfaction
of the concerned Trial Court.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

RAVISH M S @ RAVI ETC.           Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF KARNATAKA              Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The appellants were convicted by the Trial
Court for the offences punishable under Sections
120-B, 302 and 201 read with Section 34 of the
Indian  Penal  Code,  1860,  arising  out  of  FIR
No.71/2019, dated 07.04.2019. The appellants filed

--- Page 159 ---
2
criminal  appeals  challenging  their  conviction
before the High Court. The applications seeking
suspension of sentence during the pendency of
their  appeals  have  been  dismissed  vide  the
impugned order.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
Admittedly, the appellants have been under
incarceration for nearly 7 years and the appeals
filed before the High Court are of the year 2025.
In such view of the matter, we find that the
decision of the High Court cannot be sustained in
the eyes of law.
Considering the above, we are inclined to set
aside the impugned order and grant bail to the
appellants.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the appellants are granted bail on terms
and  conditions  to  the  satisfaction  of  the
concerned Trial Court.

--- Page 160 ---
3
The appeals stand allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL/CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.360 OF 2025
ANJU SHARMA & ANR.                       PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
DIPEN SHARMA                       RESPONDENT(S)
WITH
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.1212 OF 2025
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.1195 OF 2025
O R D E R
1. Transfer Petition (Criminal) No.360 of 2025  has been
filed  seeking  transfer  of  Maintenance  Petition  bearing
MNT/125/47/2023 titled "Anju Sharma & Anr. vs. Dipen Sharma ”,
pending before the Court of the Principal Judge, Family Court,
Sirsa, Haryana to the Principal Judge, Family Court Jaipur,
Rajasthan.
2. Transfer Petition (Civil) No.1212 of 2025 has been filed
by the petitioner-wife seeking transfer of HMA/1172/2023 titled
"Anju Sharma vs. Dipen Sharma”, pending before the Court of the
Principal Judge, Family Court, Sirsa, Haryana to the Principal
Judge, Family Court Jaipur, Rajasthan.
1

--- Page 169 ---
3. Transfer Petition (Civil) No.1195 of 2025 has been filed
by the petitioner-wife seeking transfer of Case No.CM/235/2022
titled "Dipen Sharma vs. Anju Sharma”, pending before the Court
of the Principal Judge, Family Court, Sirsa, Haryana to the
Principal Judge, Family Court Jaipur, Rajasthan.
4. Learned counsel appearing for the petitioner-wife states
that there are arrears to the tune of Rs.1,55,000/- (Rupees one
lakh fifty five thousand).  Learned counsel for the respondent-
husband states that arrears, if any, shall be cleared within a
period of two weeks.
5. The arrears of maintenance having computed based on the
orders  obtaining  now  and  any  future  proceeding  will  be
uninfluenced by the observations in this order.
6. As far as the visitation rights are concerned, learned
counsel for the petitioner states that absolutely there is no
objection to comply with the orders made by the Family Court.
It transpires that under the order, the visitation was to
happen at the Mediation Centre attached to the Family Court,
Sirsa, Haryana.
7. The  petitioner-wife  and  the  respondent-husband  may
mutually agree upon any other child friendly place.  Learned
counsel for the petitioner-wife undertakes that the order will
be duly complied with.
2

--- Page 170 ---
8. It is also desirable that the child interacts with both
parents for wholesome development of the child.  The parties
may mutually agree and facilitate video calls also at the
mutually agreed time and mutually agreed date.
9. Having heard learned counsel appearing for the parties,
we  are  inclined  to  transfer  the  cases  sought  for.  We,
accordingly, allow the transfer petitions and direct that  (i)
Maintenance  Petition  bearing  MNT/125/47/2023  titled  "Anju
Sharma & Anr. vs. Dipen Sharma ”, (ii) HMA/1172/2023 titled
"Anju Sharma vs. Dipen Sharma ” and (iii) Case No.CM/235/2022
titled "Dipen Sharma vs. Anju Sharma”  pending before the Court
of the Principal Judge, Family Court, Sirsa, Haryana shall be
transferred  to  the  Principal  Judge,  Family  Court  Jaipur,
Rajasthan.
10. However, if the video conferencing facility is available
with the Court, the benefit of the same shall be extended to
the parties, unless their physical presence is required.
11. The transferor Court shall forthwith transmit the record
of the aforesaid case to the transferee court.
3

--- Page 171 ---
12. The transferee Court shall make all possible endeavours
first for an amicable settlement between the parties, failing
which for early decision in the matter without unnecessary
adjournments.
13. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 2982 OF 2025
RENUKA SINGH … PETITIONER
VERSUS
RAN VIJAY YADAV … RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. The petitioner (wife) has filed this transfer
petition seeking transfer of Petition No. 773 of 2025
titled as Ranvijay Yadav vs. Renuka Singh filed by the
respondent (husband) under Section 13(1) of the Hindu
Marriage Act, 1955, from the court of the Principal
Judge, Family Court, Azamgarh, Uttar Pradesh to the

--- Page 174 ---
2
court of the Principal Judge, Tis Hazari Courts, Delhi.
2. During the course of hearing, it was informed that
the  petitioner  is  staying  in  Delhi  since  she  is
preparing for UPSC examination though her parents are
staying at Lucknow.  No document indicating that she is
taking coaching of IAS or otherwise application for
appearing in UPSC examination has been brought on
record  except  filing  of  a  rent  agreement.   The
proceedings are pending in Azamgarh and, as contended
before us, the respondent is pursuing his MBBS course
at Kanpur.   
4. Considering  all  attending  circumstance  and  in
absence of any cogent document regarding the study of
IAS by the petitioner in Delhi, considering her parents
are staying in Lucknow, we deem it appropriate to
transfer  the  case  from  Azamgarh  to  Lucknow.
Accordingly,  Petition No. 773 of 2025 titled as
Ranvijay Yadav vs. Renuka Singh filed by the respondent
(husband) under Section 13(1) of the Hindu Marriage

--- Page 175 ---
3
Act, 1955, is ordered to be transferred from the court
of the Principal Judge, Family Court, Azamgarh, Uttar
Pradesh to the court of the Principal Judge, Family
Court, Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh.
5. It is on the discretion of the transferee Court to
allow  the  presence  of  the  respondent  (husband)
virtually until necessarily required.
6. On intimation by e-mail, the records of the case
be transferred to the transferee Court without any
delay and immediate compliance be made.
7. The transfer petition is, accordingly, allowed.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
CIVIL APPEAL NO. ______ OF 2026
[SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (C) NO. _________ OF 2026]
[Diary No(s). 27798 OF 2026]
BHAUSAHEB TUKARAM GOJARE & ORS.              Appellant (s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRA & ORS.              Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. This appeal is filed against the impugned interim
order dated 26.03.2026 in Writ Petition No. 2275 of
2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at Bombay,
Bench at Aurangabad.
3. By the impugned order, the High Court passed the
following directions :-
“(a)  In  all  districts  where  orders
pursuant  to  the  impugned  Government
Resolution dated 20th February 2026 have
already been issued and Administrators
and  administrative  committees  have
already  taken  charge,  such
Administrators  and  administrative
committees  shall  confine  themselves
strictly  to  routine  day-to-day
administrative functions. The matters in
respect of which they may act include,
payment  of  salaries  and  wages  to
employees, labourers and workers of the
Gram Panchayat; payment of water charges
and  electricity  charges;  payment  of
maintenance  expenses;  and  all  other
essential statutory payments required to
maintain  essential  services  at  the
village level. Such persons who have
taken charge may continue to perform

--- Page 179 ---
2
these limited functions until further
orders. 
(b)  Subject  to  the  above,  the
Administrators  and  administrative
committees  appointed  pursuant  to  the
impugned  Government  Resolution  shall
not,  during  the  pendency  of  these
petitions and until further orders of
this Court, take any policy decision,
incur  any  major  financial  liability,
create any third-party rights, award any
fresh contracts or tenders, or take any
decision having long-term financial or
administrative consequences for the Gram
Panchayat or for the public at large. 
(c) In all districts where, as on 27th
February 2026, no orders pursuant to the
impugned Government Resolution had been
issued or no charge had been taken by
any  Administrator  or  administrative
committee, no further steps shall be
taken pursuant to the said Government
Resolution  dated  20th  February  2026
until further orders of this Court.
(d) The above directions shall operate
as a modification of and in substitution
of  the  earlier  ad-interim  directions
issued by this Court on 27th February
2026, bringing them in uniformity with
the order dated 18th March 2026 of the
coordinate  Division  Bench  at  the
Principal Seat.
(e) The State respondents are granted
time to file a detailed affidavit in
reply in all petitions where the same
has  not  yet  been  filed.  Rejoinder
affidavits, if any, may be filed by the
petitioners thereafter.”
4. Mr.  Siddharth  Dharmadhikari,  learned  counsel
appearing for the State of Maharashtra, submitted that
insofar as Chhatrapati Sambhaji Nagar District is
concerned, orders were, in fact, prepared but could
not be issued by 27.02.2026.  He would further submit

--- Page 180 ---
3
that there would be difficulty in good governance if
there is no Administrator for Chhatrapati Sambhaji
Nagar District. He has therefore requested that this
Court may appropriately modify the order to enable
proper and effective administration of justice.
5. Having heard learned counsel for the appellants
and the respondents and having considered the matter
in detail, we deem it appropriate to modify the order
impugned  before  us,  by  directing  appointment  of
Administrator  for  the  Chhatrapati  Sambhaji  Nagar
District  and  issue  necessary  Notification.  The
Administrator  so  appointed  shall  abide  by  the
directions issued by the High Court in the order
impugned before us.  
6. It  is  made  clear  that  except  the  above
modification, the order passed by the High Court shall
remain unaltered.
7. With these observations and directions, the civil
appeal stands disposed of.
8. Pending  interlocutory  application(s),  if  any,
stands disposed of.   
……………………………………………………………………….J.
[PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA]
…………………………………………………………………...J.
    [ALOK ARADHE]
NEW DELHI;
MAY 20, 2026.

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  04-09-2025

M/S MARK BUILDCON & ORS.                           Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRA & ORS.                    Respondent(s)
(FOR ADMISSION and I.R. IA No. 307210/2025 - PERMISSION TO FILE

Brief note on behalf of the Petitioner
1. It is submitted at the outset that the
petitioner does not deny the “entitlement”

Management and Transfer) Act, 1963, for the
“B-Wing” of the Project.
2. The issue is however of the “extent” of
which  such  deemed  conveyance  can  be
granted. It is submitted that:-
(a) The order dated 17.3.2025 ( page 25-31)

(b) Based on the order, a certificate dated

Documents)

as per UDCPR 2020 by purchasing and
loading additional TDR.

                             O R D E R
Heard Ms. Adya Shree Dutta, learned counsel for the
petitioners  and  Mr.  Navaniti  Prasad  Singh,  learned
Senior Counsel for the respondents. 
2. There is an inordinate delay in filing of these
3

--- Page 191 ---
DIARY NO. 11563/2025
petitions by the petitioners without any sufficient or
satisfactory cause being shown. 
3. We have considered the matters in depth. The issue
relates to the allegation that the respondents have cut
the trees illegally. The specific allegation is that the
respondents were given permission to cut certain number
of trees from their own land, but they had also cut the
trees, which were in the protected forest area.
4. After  going  through  the  material  on  record,
especially the statement made in the rejoinder affidavit
filed by the petitioners indicating that the trees which
were stated to have been cut by the respondents, were
actually not taken away by them, but found at the spot,
for which the Government has held the officers of the
concerned department responsible and cases have been
instituted  against  them,  the  Court  finds  that  no
interference is required in the common impugned order.
5. Accordingly,  the  Special  Leave  Petitions  stand
dismissed both on the ground of delay as well as on
merits. 
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
  (POOJA SHARMA)                            (ANJALI PANWAR)

--- Page 196 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL  NO.         OF 2026
(@ SLP(C) No.12671/2026)
  DIRECTORATE OF ENFORCEMENT                         APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
M/S ZO PRIVATE LIMITED                             RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The short question arising for consideration is as to
whether the High Court was right in exercising its
discretion by permitting the withdrawal of funds from the
frozen bank accounts for payment of the respondent’s
employees’ salaries while relegating the respondent to
contest the proceedings against the continuance of the
order of freezing passed under Section 17(1A) of the
Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002 before the
Adjudicating Authority. 
3. The learned ASG appearing for the appellant states that
such a practice should not be approved, particularly, in
light of the law laid down by this Court. 
4. Mr. Rohatgi, learned Senior counsel appearing for the
respondent submitted that one has to see the offence as
alleged against the parent company and we are only
dealing with the subsidiary company here. The High Court

--- Page 197 ---
2
has rightly taken note of the relevant facts while
exercising  such  discretion  which  is  to  permit  the
withdrawal  of  funds  for  the  purpose  of  payment  of
salaries to the employees alone.
5. In our considered view, after relegating the respondent
to exercise the statutory remedy before the Adjudicating
Authority, the High Court ought not to have exercised
such a discretion and the said discretion cannot be
countenanced.
6. In such view of the matter, we are inclined to set aside
the impugned order to the extent of such discretion
exercised by the High Court and hold that all issues are
left open to be decided by the Adjudicating Authority and
therefore, any observations made by the High Court shall
not stand in the way.
7. Thus,  the  impugned  order  stands  set  aside  to  the
aforesaid extent. We do take note of the grievance
expressed by the learned Senior counsel appearing for the
respondent that the discretion has been exercised for the
welfare of the employees alone. 
8. Without expressing anything on merits, we direct the
Adjudicating Authority to conclude the proceedings one
way or another, in accordance with law and as per the
mandate of the statute.
9. The  appeal  stands  disposed  of  with  the  aforesaid
observations. 

--- Page 198 ---
3
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of. 
  
  ……………………………………………………………………J.
  [M.M. SUNDRESH]
       ………………………………………………………………………J.
    [NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH]
NEW DELHI;
15th MAY, 2026
