--- Page 200 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL  NO.             OF 2026
(@ SLP(C) No.17644/2026)
M/S XCLUSIVE INN PRIVATE LIMITED & ANR.            APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
ANJANA GUHA & ORS.                                 RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted. 
2. This appeal arises from the judgment dated 09.04.2026
passed by the High Court of Calcutta (‘the High Court’)
whereby the first appeal preferred by the appellants was
dismissed and the judgment and decree dated 10.07.2023
passed by the Trial Court was affirmed.
3. The facts giving rise to the present appeal, briefly
stated,  are  that  the  appellants  were  inducted  into
possession of the premises to run a hotel and restaurant
business on a monthly rent of Rs.28,000/- for a period of
ten years ending on 16.03.2018. Thereafter, the agreement
was renewed.  
4. In March 2021, the respondents instituted a suit seeking
recovery of possession, arrears of rent/licence fee, and
mesne profits. The appellants contested the suit,  inter
alia,  contending  that  they  had  not  been  inducted  as
licensees but as tenants, whose tenancy rights had not
been validly determined by issuance of a notice under

--- Page 201 ---
2
Section 106 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882.
5.  The Trial Court, by judgment and decree dated 10.07.2023,
inter  alia,  held that  in  the  written  statement,  the
appellants had admitted the licence fee as well as the
fact that period of licence has expired. Relying upon the
pleadings in the plaint and invoking the provisions of
Order XII Rule 6 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, the
Trial Court decreed the suit. 
6. Aggrieved thereby, the appellants preferred a first appeal
before the High Court. The High Court, by the impugned
judgment, dismissed the appeal. 
7. Learned senior counsel appearing for the appellants has
raised a singular contention, namely, that the High Court,
while deciding the first appeal in a summary manner,
failed to consider or deal with the submissions advanced
on behalf of the appellants. Per contra, learned counsel
for the respondents has supported the judgment passed by
the High Court. 
8. It is trite law that the right to prefer a first appeal
against a decree is a valuable legal right vested in a
litigant. The jurisdiction of the first appellate court is
extensive and coextensive with that of the Trial Court.
It is open to the appellants to assail findings of fact as
well as law in such an appeal. It is, therefore, incumbent
upon  the  first  appellate  court  to  independently
appreciate the material available on record and arrive at

--- Page 202 ---
3
its own conclusions supported by cogent reasons. 
9.  Upon perusal of the memorandum of appeal preferred by the
appellants and the impugned judgment passed by the High
Court, we find that neither the submissions advanced on
behalf of the appellants nor the grounds urged in the
memorandum of appeal have been adverted to by the High
Court. The High Court has dismissed the first appeal in a
cursory manner without considering the submissions and
grounds raised by the appellants and without recording any
independent findings or conclusions. The High Court has
merely concurred with the findings recorded by the Trial
Court and affirmed its judgment and decree.
10.For the aforementioned reasons, the judgment and decree
passed by the High Court is set aside and the appeal is
remitted for consideration afresh. Pending disposal of the
appeal, interim order passed on 17.08.2023 by the High
Court shall continue. We request the High Court to decide
the appeal within a period of three months. 
11. Accordingly, the appeal is disposed of.
12. Pending application (s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
 
  
  ……………………………………………………………………J.
             [PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA]
       ………………………………………………………………………J.
        [ALOK ARADHE]
NEW DELHI;
21st MAY, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
 
 
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO.1361 OF 2026 
 
 
IN 
 
 
SPECIAL LEA VE PETITION (CIVIL) NO.35888 OF 2025 
 
 
 
NITIN GORDHANBHAI THAKKER  & ORS.      …       APPLICANT(S) 
 
 
 
VERSUS 
 
 
JAGANNATH GIRI                                                 …   RESPONDENT(S) 
 
 
 
O R D E R 
 
 
1. A long-standing litigation with respect to an unauthorised 
encroachment on a property of Babulnath Temple Trust1, came to an end, 
upon dismissal of the SLP preferred by the occupant Jagannath Giri , who 
was, vide order dated 19.12.2025 permitted by this Court , to occupy the 
premises for a period of four years and with the expiration of the said period 
hand over the premises to the Trust.  The present applicant(s)-Trust by way 
 
1 Hereinafter ‘Trust’ 

--- Page 209 ---
of the instant application seeks modification of the said order in terms of the 
following prayer:- 
 
(i) Allow the present Application and clarify that (i) Original 
Petitioner is not a tenant, (ii) the Original Petitioner’s continued 
occupation during the four -year period is expressly understood to 
be subject to the above basic conditions (in substance); and (iii) it 
is clarified that the Original petitioner undertakes to hand over 
vacant, peaceful and unconditional possession of the concerned 
place to the Respondent-Temple Trust on competition of four years 
from 19th December 2025, enabling effective implementation of the 
SC Order without further contest” 
 
 
2. Having heard learned counsel for the parties we pass the following 
directions:-  
 
(i)  We partially modify the said order dated 19.12.2025 clarifying 
that the original petitioner Jagannath Giri2, is neither a tenant nor a 
permissive occupier in his own right under the authority of the Trust. 
Save and except permission granted to him to occupy the property in 
terms of and by virtue of orders passed by this Court he has no right 
in law;   
(ii) Under no circumstances any third person /party, be it his 
disciple; or chela; or sevak would be entitled to occupy the premises, 
if the original  petitioner leaves for the heavenly abode prior to the 
expiry of the aforesaid period.  In such a case, the permission granted 
by this Court shall stand automatically extinguished; 
 
(iii) Clarifying further, this Court has only permitted the petitioner 
and one of his companions to occupy the premises till the expiry of 
the abovesaid period i.e., 19 th December 2029  or any eventuality 
arising prior thereto .  No one  else, through him or independently  
 
2 Petitioner in SLP(C)No.35888 of 2025 referred to as ‘Petitioner’ 

--- Page 210 ---
(except the Trust)  has any right, title or interest in the demised 
premises. The limited permission to occupy the premises, granted out 
of humanitarian consideration, was solely to enable him to pursue his 
religious and spiritual activit ies in the evening of his life .  The 
applicant’s apprehension being that just like the predecessor in 
interest of the petitioner, his successor in interest or chela may claim 
right to continue to occupy the demised premises and pursue the 
religious/spiritual activity therefrom.  We do hereby clarify that none 
other than the petitioner , has been accorded this concession of 
occupying the premises; 
 
(iv) It is further clarified that the petitioner Jagannath Giri, shall 
not commence and/or carry out any other new activity. Also, he shall 
not carry out the activity of cooking or distribution of “ prasad”  as 
“bhandara” from the demised premises.  Any cooking activity to be 
undertaken by Jagannath Giri, shall be confined only to cater to his 
and/or his helper’s personal needs;      
  
(v) The Trust wants to install an escalator on the staircase, part of 
which is in the demised premises, we clarify that as and when such 
an activity is commenced , the petitioner shall ensure and facilitate 
smooth execution of such work without causing any hindrance;    
  
(vi) The Trust wants the petitioner to remove the “dhooni”, which 
request we are not inclined to accept for the same being part of the 
religious activity continuously performed by him; and 
 
(vii) The petitioner  shall not expand his possession or carry out 
encroachment beyond the boundaries of the suit premises.   He will 

--- Page 211 ---
ensure that no obstruction is caused to the devotees visiting the 
temple.   
 
  3. The order dated 19.12.2025 is modified to the aforesaid extent.  With 
the above observations application stands disposed of.  The petitioner shall 
immediately furnish an undertaking in the aforesaid terms not later than 
seven days from the date of this order being uploaded on the website.   
 
 
 
 
……………………………………..………J. 
(SANJAY KAROL) 
 
 
 
 
………………………………………..……J. 
(NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH)    
 
New Delhi 
13th May 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO. 5425/2026]
MOHAMMED SHAMEEM               Appellant(s)
                     VERSUS
STATE OF KERALA                 Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The  appellant  seeks  to  challenge  the
cancellation  of  bail,  in  connection  with  FIR
No.405/2024,  dated  24.05.2024,  registered  at
Police Station Karipur, District – Malappuram, for
the offence punishable under Section 22(b) of the
Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act,
1985.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
The bail granted to the appellant has been
cancelled on the premise that he was involved in a
1

--- Page 221 ---
second case.
The fact remains that in the second case, the
appellant has been granted bail, with observations
on infirmities in the case of the prosecution.
Considering the above, we are inclined to set
aside  the  impugned  order  and  direct  for  the
release of the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the appellant is directed to be released
forthwith, unless required in any other case, on
terms and conditions to the satisfaction of the
concerned Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
...................J.

1
TP (C) No. 145 of 2026
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) No. 145 of 2026
HARMANPREET KAUR … PETITIONER
VERSUS
NAVJOT SINGH … RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1 The petitioner (wife) has filed this petition seeking
transfer of HMA No. 1035 of 2025 titled as Navjot Singh vs.
Harmanpreet Kaur  filed by the respondent (husband) under
Section 13 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, from the court
of the Additional Principal Judge, Family Court, Amritsar,
Punjab to the court of the Principal Judge, Family Court,
Kullu, Himachal Pradesh.
2. Despite service of notice on the respondent, no one
has entered appearance on his behalf.  However, vide order
dated  23.04.2026,  awaiting  representation  of  the
respondent, the matter was adjourned.  Even today, there is

--- Page 229 ---
2
TP (C) No. 145 of 2026
no one present on behalf of the respondent to oppose the
prayer as made in the transfer petition.
3. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner and
looking to the distance between Amritsar, Punjab and Kullu,
Himachal  Pradesh,  it  would  be  inconvenient  to  the
petitioner (wife) to attend the proceedings at Amritsar,
Punjab.
4. Therefore,  we  deem  it  appropriate  to  allow  this
transfer petition and accordingly, HMA No. 1035 of 2025
titled as Navjot Singh vs. Harmanpreet Kaur  filed by the
respondent (husband) under Section 13 of the Hindu Marriage
Act, 1955, is ordered to be transferred from the court of
the Additional Principal Judge, Family Court, Amritsar,
Punjab to the court of the Principal Judge, Family Court,
Kullu, Himachal Pradesh.
5. It is on the discretion of the transferee Court to
allow the presence of the respondent (husband) virtually
until necessarily required.
6. On intimation by email, the records of the case be
transferred to the transferee Court without any delay and
immediate compliance be made. confirmed

--- Page 230 ---
3
TP (C) No. 145 of 2026
7. The transfer petition is, accordingly, allowed.
8. Pending applications, if any, stand disposed of.
…………………………………………………,J.
[J.K. MAHESHWARI]
…………………………………………………,J.
[ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
New Delhi;
May 21, 2026.

1. The petitioner (wife) has filed this petition seeking
transfer of HMA No. 1957 of 2025 titled as Vishal Sharma

Section 13(1)(i-a) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 from the
court of the Principal Judge, Family Court, Agra, Uttar
Pradesh to the court of the Principal Judge, Family Court,
East District, Karkardooma District Courts, Delhi.
2. Despite service of notice on the respondent, no one
has entered appearance on his behalf.  However, vide order
dated  09.04.2026,  awaiting  representation  of  the
respondent, the matter was adjourned.  Even today, there is

--- Page 233 ---
2
TP (C) No. 176 of 2026
no one present on behalf of the respondent to oppose the
prayer as made in the transfer petition.
3. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner and
looking to the distance between Agra, Uttar Pradesh and
Delhi, it would be inconvenient to the petitioner (wife) to
attend the proceedings at Agra, Uttar Pradesh.
4. Therefore,  we  deem  it  appropriate  to  allow  this
transfer petition and accordingly, HMA No. 1957 of 2025
titled as  Vishal Sharma vs. Barkha Sharma  filed by the
respondent (husband) under Section 13(1)(i-a) of the Hindu
Marriage Act, 1955 is ordered to be transferred from the
court of the Principal Judge, Family Court, Agra, Uttar
Pradesh to the court of the Principal Judge, Family Court,
East District, Karkardooma District Courts, Delhi.
5. It is on the discretion of the transferee Court to
allow the presence of the respondent (husband) virtually
until necessarily required.
6. On intimation by email, the records of the case be
transferred to the transferee Court without any delay and
immediate compliance be made. confirmed

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 2677 OF 2025
VAISHALI … PETITIONER
VERSUS
VIKRANT … RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. The petitioner (wife) has filed this transfer
petition seeking transfer of Family Suit No. 46 of 2025
titled as Vikrant vs. Vaishali filed by the respondent
(husband under Section 13(1)(i-a) and (i-b)of the Hindu
Marriage Act, 1955, from the Family Court at Rajkot,
Gujarat to the Family Court at Pune, Maharashtra.

--- Page 237 ---
2
2. After hearing learned counsel for the parties,
considering the distance between Rajkot, Gujarat and
Pune, Maharashtra and also the fact that a three years
girl child is with the wife who is suffering from
Eczema and Pityriasis Capitis, in our view, it would be
inconvenient to the petitioner (wife) to attend the
proceedings at Rajkot, Gujarat
4. Therefore, we deem it appropriate to allow this
transfer petition and accordingly, Family Suit No. 46
of 2025 titled as Vikrant vs. Vaishali  filed by the
respondent (husband under Section 13(1)(i-a) and (I-
b)of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, is ordered to be
transferred from the Family Court at Rajkot, Gujarat to
the Family Court at Pune, Maharashtra
5. The respondent (husband) is permitted to appear
before  the  transferee  court  virtually  until  his
presence is necessarily required.

--- Page 238 ---
3
6. On intimation by e-mail, the records of the case
be transferred to the transferee Court without any
delay and immediate compliance be made.
7. The transfer petition is, accordingly, allowed.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

For Respondent(s) :

                   Mr. Sameer Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Rashi Agnihotry, Adv.
                   Mr. Anukirat Singh Baweja, Adv.
                   
                   
1

--- Page 249 ---
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Pursuant to our order dated 20.05.2026, Ms.
Kunti  Singhal,  a  Counsellor  in  the  panel  of
Mediation Centre of this Court, is present along
with learned counsel for the parties.
2. Mr Kunti informed us that she could interact
with  the  child  for  an  extra  hour.  From  her
interaction with the child, she could ascertain
that there are some emotional issues with the child
having regard to the past incidents though the
child is comfortable in the environment in which he
is placed. We also inquired from her whether the
child  could  be  considered  for  placement  in  a
boarding school. On this issue, she submitted that
the  child  would  have  to  be  made  prepared  for
admission in a boarding school.
3. The leaned counsel for the petitioner, based
on  her  instructions,  has  submitted  that  the
petitioner side is not averse to the idea of the
child being admitted in a boarding school. They,
however, would be recommending admission in Duhan
Public School, Rohtak instead of the other two.
2

--- Page 250 ---
4. Based on the above input, we are of the view
that the child needs opportunity to be in a neutral
environment. The respondent is also prepared to
bear the educational expenses of the child at Duhan
Public School. However, the respondent submits that
to prepare the child for admission in a boarding
school, the respondent  would need an extra day of
interaction every week with the child so that he
could  be prepared  for admission  in a  boarding
school.
5. Having  regard  to  above,  we  deem  it
appropriate  to  modify  our  earlier  order  dated
02.12.2025  so  as  to  enable  the  respondent  to
interact with the child on Saturday and Sunday
every week. Having regard to the summer months and
the extreme heat conditions that are prevailing,
we are of the view that the meeting time of the
respondent with the child can be shifted towards
the evening starting from 4:30 pm to 8:30 pm on
Saturday and Sunday of the week.  We also deem it
appropriate to direct that respondent to complete
the formalities for admission of the child at Duhan
Public School, Rohtak. To ensure that necessary
formalities  for  admission  are  completed  by  the
respondent, the petitioner shall provide all the
relevant  documents  such  as  School  Leaving
3

--- Page 251 ---
Certificate, Transfer Certificate, Mark-sheets etc.
to the respondent. Such documents may be provided
by 26.05.2026.
6. List this matter on 31.07.2026 in Chambers
at 2:00pm. On the next date the child shall be
produced either by the petitioner or persons in
whose custody the child is placed.
(CHETAN ARORA)                                  (SAPNA BANSAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
PETITION FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (C)  NO.  38396/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  07-11-2025
in MATAPP(FC) No. 394/2025 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New
Delhi]
REETIKA                                            PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
RAJINDER KUMAR JINDAL & ANR.                       RESPONDENT(S)
IA No. 336865/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT, IA No. 336866/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T., IA No.
336867/2025  -  PERMISSION  TO  FILE  ADDITIONAL  DOCUMENTS/FACTS/
ANNEXURES
 
Date : 21-05-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s) :Ms. Anjana Prakash, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Rohit Kumar Singh, AoR
                   Mr. Mohit Chadha, Adv.
                   Ms. Jaishree Bhadauria, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Vijay Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Prakash Kumar Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Ashwini Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Prasanna Mohan, Adv.
                   Mr. Vishnu Kant Pandey, Adv.
                   Mr. Niteen Kumar Sinha, AoR                   
                   
                             O R D E R
Heard learned Senior Counsel for the petitioner and
learned counsel for the respondents. 
1

--- Page 264 ---
SLP (C) NO. 38396/2025
2. By way of the present Special Leave Petition, the
petitioner/mother  of  the  girl  child  challenged  the
impugned order dated 07.11.2025 passed in MAT.APP(F.C)
394/2024  wherein  the  High  Court  of  Delhi  refused  to
interfere with the order of the Family Court dismissing
the  Guardianship  Petition  filed  by  the  respondents/
paternal  grandparents,  however,  granting  limited
visitation  rights  to  the  respondents  to  meet  their
granddaughter, i.e., the girl child. 
3. From the previous orders passed by this Court, it is
clear that upon repeated requests made by learned Senior
Counsel for the petitioner, the Court had deferred the
meeting of the respondents with the child by granting
interim stay of the impugned order. However, today, again
a  prayer  is  made  for  delaying  the  visitation  rights
granted to the respondents on some pretext or the other. 
4. We find that the order passed by the High Court is
just, fair and equitable, more so, since the child shall
be attaining the age of 18 years, i.e., majority in
August, 2026.
5. In view of the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petition
stands dismissed.
6. Interim order passed by this Court on 08.01.2026
stands withdrawn. Parties to comply with the order of the
High Court, forthwith. 
2

--- Page 265 ---
SLP (C) NO. 38396/2025
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of. 
 (POOJA SHARMA)                                (ANJALI PANWAR)
    AR-CUM-PS                               ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. At the outset, Mr. Navaniti Prasad Singh, learned
Senior Counsel appearing for the petitioner, assisted by
1

--- Page 270 ---
SLP (C)  NO.  13570/2020
Mr. Vaibhav Niti, learned counsel, has taken the Court to
the affidavit filed by the petitioner and the response
thereto by the respondent. Two sets of the settlement
terms have been brought on record by the parties, which
they claim to be the correct version of what was agreed
upon between them during the negotiations, inter se.
3. Be that as it may, learned Senior Counsel for the
petitioner  submitted  that  the  discrepancy  basically
relates to: (i) whether a 3 bedroom flat or a 2 bedroom
flat was agreed between the parties; and (ii) the quantum
of monthly maintenance, whether it was Rs.20,000/- (Rupees
Twenty Thousand) or Rs.40,000/- (Rupees Forty Thousand).
4. At this juncture, learned Senior Counsel for the
petitioner  submitted  that  he  undertakes  that  the
petitioner would readily agree to give a 3 bedroom flat to
the respondent. However, he submitted that in spite of
whatever  concession  the  petitioner  gives,  the  real
intention of the respondent is to harass him, for the
reason that at the end her stand is that whatever may be
the settlement, but still she will not give divorce to the
petitioner.
5. On a response sought from learned counsel for the
respondent upon the Court tentatively observing that there
appears  to  be  unreasonableness  on  the  part  of  the
respondent, time is sought to take instructions.
2

--- Page 271 ---
SLP (C)  NO.  13570/2020
6. Accordingly, as prayed for by learned counsel for the
respondent, list on 14.08.2026.
7. In the meantime, interim order passed by this Court on
19.03.2025 and extended from time to time to continue till
the next date of listing.

         UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
IA No. 6307/2026 
1. This application is ordered and the Mumbai Metropolitan Region
Development Authority is brought on record as respondent No.5.
2. Learned counsel appearing for the said authority seeks time to
file a counter affidavit.
3. Needful shall be done within four weeks from today.
4. Rejoinder(s) thereto, if any, may be filed within two weeks
thereafter.
5. In the meanwhile, as we have already permitted the DB Ozone
Home Buyers Association to intervene in this matter and we are
informed  that,  apart  from  the  1,100  members  who  form  this
association, there are several other home buyers, 3,300 in number,
who are in occupation of the buildings but are not in a position to
enjoy their properties fully for want of an occupancy certificate
from the  Mira Bhayander  Municipal Corporation. Irrespective of
whatever issues the said corporation and the newly impleaded
authority may have with the developer, it is not open to these
authorities to hold to ransom the innocent home buyers, who are in
2

--- Page 274 ---
occupation of the apartments in these buildings. The authority and
the corporation shall, therefore, undertake an inspection of the
completed buildings and submit a report to this Court as to whether
there  are  any  grounds  to  withhold  issuance  of  an  occupancy
certificate/partial occupancy certificate, other than the issues
that they seek to raise against the developer.
6. Further, the authority and the corporation shall also ensure
that basic amenities are provided to the occupants of these
apartments.
7. The Report, in terms of this order, shall be filed by the
corporation and the authority within six weeks. Advance copies
thereof shall be supplied to the counsel appearing in this matter.
8. Re-list the special leave petition on 27.08.2026.
IA No. 114943/2026
1.  We find no reason to entertain this intervention application
filed by a third party who is not affected by this litigation.
2. The application is, accordingly, dismissed.
(IRAMNAZ)                                       (PREETI SAXENA)

the matter to be heard and the same being done, the Registry has

dismissed by this Court on 30.10.2023, the same has to be returned
to the respondent(s)/decree holder(s).

Rs.50,000/-  (Rupees  Fifty  Thousand)  to  the  respondent(s),  in
accordance with law.

ITEM NO.31               COURT NO.6               SECTION XIV-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Civil Appeal  No(s).  757-760/2013
D.P.C.C.                                           Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA THROUGH SECRETARY,                 Respondent(s)
[MS. SHIBANI GHOSH AND MR. NINAD LAUD, ADVOCATES ARE APPOINTED AS 
AMICI CURIAE] 
IA No. 225827/2025 - INTERVENTION/IMPLEADMENT
 
WITH
C.A. No. 1977-2011/2013 (XIV-A)
IA No. 205979/2025 - INTERVENTION/IMPLEADMENT
 
Date : 21-05-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ALOK ARADHE
For Appellant(s)    Ms. Shibani Ghosh, Adv. (AC)
Mr. Ninad Laud, Adv. (AC)
Mr. Pradeep Misra, AOR
                   Mr. Daleep Dhyani, Adv.
                   Mr. Anupam Misra, Adv.
                   Mr. Suraj Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Dinesh Jindal, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Archana Pathak Dave, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Gurmeet Singh Makker, AOR
                   Ms. Harshita Choubey, Adv.
                   Mr. Kamal Digpaul, Adv.
                   Mr. Bhuvan Kapoor, Adv.
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. By our judgment and order dated 04.08.2026, we had given the
following directions:-
”35.  To  ensure  that  the  Boards  impose
restitutionary  and  the  compensatory
environmental damages in a fair transparent,

--- Page 294 ---
2
nonarbitrary  manner,  with  procedural
certainty, necessary subordinate legislation
in the form of rules and regulations must be
notified. This shall include methods by which
environmental damage is determined, and the
consequent quantum of damages are assessed.
They  may  also  incorporate  certain  basic
principles of natural justice for fairness in
action. At present environmental damages are
being levied by the Boards on the basis of
certain  guidelines  issued  by  the  Central
Pollution  Control  Board  in  its  document
“General framework for imposing environmental
damage compensation” issue in December, 2022.
These guidelines seem to have been issued
pursuant  to  the  directions  of  the  NGT
(Pursuant to the NGT in its order in O.A. No.
606/2018 dated 24.04.2019). It is important
that these guidelines are reviewed thoroughly
and  issued  in  the  form  of  Rules  and
Regulations. This will enable declaration of a
law that applies and ensures its recognition
and easy implementation.”
xxxxxxxxx
  “39(c) it is further directed that the power to
impose or collect restitutionary or compensatory
damages  or  the  requirement  to  furnish  bank
guarantees as an ex-ante measure under Sections
33A and 31A of the Water and Air Acts shall be
enforced only after 37 detailing the principle
and procedure incorporating basic principles of
natural justice in the subordinate legislation.”

--- Page 295 ---
3
2. The above directions should not be understood to mean that the
regulators, being the Pollution Control Boards cannot proceed
to impose and collect the compensatory environmental damages
in the absence of subordinate legislation, on the subject. 
3. This clarification is necessary because the learned Amicus
Curiae have brought to our notice that certain High Courts
have  been  passed  interim  orders  staying  proceedings  for
imposition and collection of environmental compensation, only
for the reason that there is no Subordinate Legislation on the
subject.
4. We reiterate that High Courts will not stay proceedings for
imposition and collection of environmental compensation only
for the reason that subordinate legislation is not made and
issued on the subject. 
5. List on 10.08.2026.
(SWETA BALODI)                                  (NIDHI WASON)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

--- Page 296 ---

O R D E R
1. This petition is filed under Section 25 of the Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908, seeking transfer of a case bearing
F.C.O.P. No. 1419 of 2024 titled  “Maddala Naga Sreenivas
Chakravarthy vs. Vaiyshnavi Kantha Rao” pending before the
Principal Family Court, Vijayawada, Andhra Pradesh to the
Family Court, Chennai, Tamil Nadu.
2. The grounds raised by the petitioner for seeking the
transfer are that she has to take care of her minor daughter
and old parents; and also on account of long distance she is
unable to effectively defend the proceedings at Vijayawada.
3. On such consideration, we are of the view that transfer
deserves to be allowed. Consequently, the Transfer Petition
is allowed.  
4. Accordingly, case bearing F.C.O.P. No. 1419 of 2024 titled
“Maddala Naga Sreenivas Chakravarthy vs. Vaiyshnavi Kantha Rao”
pending before the Principal Family Court, Vijayawada, Andhra
Pradesh is transferred to the  Family Court, Chennai, Tamil
Nadu.
1

--- Page 297 ---
5. The transferor Court shall forward the records pertaining to
the said case to the transferee Court, as expeditiously as
possible.
6. Parties are directed to appear before the concerned Court on
13th July, 2026.
7. The  respondent-husband  is  permitted  to  attend  the
proceedings virtually, unless his presence is required by the
trial Court. 
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).13510/2025

ROOMALI & ORS.                                     Petitioner(s)

STATE OF RAJASTHAN                                 Respondent(s)
(IA No. 84806/2026 - APPLICATION FOR EXEMPTION FROM FILING TYPED
DOCUMENTS
IA No. 84805/2026 - APPLICATION FOR EXTENSION OF INTERIM PROTECTION
IA No. 168742/2025 - APPROPRIATE ORDERS/DIRECTIONS
IA No. 313076/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 168746/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 112794/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 63843/2026 - SUSPENSION OF SENTENCE)
 
Date : 22-05-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Aarif Ali, Adv.
                   Mr. Pankaj Tiwari, Adv.
                   Mr. Chand Qureshi, AoR
                   Mr. Mujahid Ahmad, Adv.
                   Mr. Rishibh, Adv.
                   Mr. Mohd Faiz Ahmed, Adv.
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Manish Tiwari, AoR                   
                   Mr. Prabhu Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Himanshu Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Prashanth Dixit, Adv.
                   Mr. Pramod Kr Singh, Adv.                   
                   
                   Ms. Aneesha Rastogi, Adv.
                   Mr. Saurabh Rajpal, Adv.
                   Ms. Nidhi Jaswal, AoR                           
                                                        
         

--- Page 303 ---
2
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. At the outset, upon going through the response affidavit filed
by the respondent-State of Rajasthan as affirmed by Mr. Nitesh
Arya,  Additional  Superintendent  of  Police,  District  Barmer,
Rajasthan, we find that the same is of no assistance to the Court.
It was required that the affidavit should be in response to the
issues raised and relating to the documents brought on record in
the Interlocutory Application No.63843 of 2026 for suspension of
sentence filed by the learned counsel for the petitioners. In the
certificate issued by the Principal of the School in which, the
petitioner no.5 is said to have studied indicates that his date of
birth was 01.02.1976 as recorded in the school admission register.
Nothing has been mentioned with regard to the authenticity of this
certificate. However, on a query of the Court, learned counsel for
the respondent-State of Rajasthan submits that it has been verified
and the same is genuine and not a forged or fabricated document.
3. The Court is at a loss to understand, as to how, the response
affidavit has been filed when there is no answer/reply/stand taken
in the affidavit with regard to the most important issue raised in
the said Interlocutory Application i.e., the documents in support
of the contention of the petitioner no.5, that he was a juvenile on
the date of the incident.
4. We find that the conduct of the respondent-State of Rajasthan
is absolutely casual and thus, unacceptable.
5. Accordingly,  let  the  Superintendent  of  Police,  District

--- Page 304 ---
3
Barmer, Rajasthan appear before the Court personally on the next
date of listing i.e., 26.05.2026, when the matter will be listed at
the top of the Board, along with his explanation, as to why, the
Court should not initiate appropriate proceedings and pass such
orders as may be required against him for such casual filing of the
response affidavit, which has absolutely not dealt with by the
Authorities concerned.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.  405/2026
KAJAL SHARMA ALIAS PINKI                           Petitioner(s)
VERSUS
VIKASH KUMAR SHARMA                                Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. This is a petition filed under Section 25 of the Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908. The petitioner has filed this Transfer
Petition seeking the following reliefs:
"Transfer the Family Main Case No. 385 of 2025 filed
by the Respondent U/s 13(1) of Hindu Marriage Act,
1955 titled as  Vikash Kumar Sharma Vs. Smt. Kajal
Sharma alias Pinki  pending before the Family Court,
Alwar,  Rajasthan  to  the  Family  Court,  Ranchi,
Jharkhand."
2. We have heard the learned counsel for both the parties. 
3. Having heard the learned counsels for the petitioner and on
perusal of the material on record, we find that the petitioner has
made out a case for transfer of the aforesaid petition. 
4. In the result, the Transfer Petition is allowed and Family
Main Case No. 385 of 2025  titled  “Vikash Kumar Sharma Vs. Smt.
Kajal Sharma alias Pinki ” pending before the Family Court, Alwar,
Rajasthan is hereby transferred  to the Family Court, Ranchi,
Jharkhand.
5. Registry to communicate this order to the transferor court
for taking steps in accordance with this order. 
6. On an application made by the respondent, the Court to which

--- Page 315 ---
the case is transferred shall permit the respondent to appear
through video-conference on all dates except on the dates on which
his personal presence is absolutely mandatory.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
WRIT ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO(S).1271 OF 2019
G V HARSHA KUMAR                                      PETITIONER
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                                RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioner
and learned Additional Solicitor General appearing for
respondent Nos.1 and 2/Union of India.
2. During  the  course  of  submissions,  learned
Additional Solicitor General brought to our notice the
fact that the Parliament has recently passed the Inland
Vessels Act, 2021 and the Rules made therein. He
therefore  submitted  that  in  view  of  the  latest
enactment and the Rules, the prayers sought for by the
petitioner herein have been rendered infructuous and
redundant.
3. We take note of the said submission and we
dispose of this Writ Petition by observing that having
regard  to  the  aforesaid  Act  and  the  Rules  made
thereunder, which is a Central Act and the Rules, it is
expected that the same shall be implemented in letter
1

--- Page 330 ---
and spirit.
4. With  the  aforesaid  observations,  the  Writ
Petition stands disposed of.
All pending application(s), including application
seeking deletion, if any, shall stand disposed of.
………………………………………………………, J
    (B.V. NAGARATHNA)
…………………………………………………………, J
  (UJJAL BHUYAN)
NEW DELHI
MAY 19, 2026
2

petition filed in HMA No.174/2024 pending on the file of

Family  Court,  Karkadooma  Complex,  (District
North East) Delhi.”
2. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioner
and learned counsel for the respondent.
3. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner
and learned counsel for the respondent and on perusal of
the material on record, we find that the petitioner has
made out a case for transfer of the aforesaid petitions.
4. In the result, the Transfer Petitions are allowed
and the Divorce Petition bearing HMA Case No.588/2022,
titled as “Sudhir v. Shashi” and the petition for
restitution  of  conjugal  rights  bearing  HMA  Case
NO.917/2022 titled as Shashi v. Sudhir, both pending

--- Page 342 ---
before the Court of Principal Judge, Family Court,
Karnal are transferred to the Family Court, Karkadooma
Complex, (District North East) Delhi.
5. The respondent is at liberty to appear either in-
person or through video conferencing facility.
6. It is needless to observe that if the respondent
seeks to appear virtually, then the competent Court
shall make available the said facility to the said
respondent.
7. Registry  to  communicate  this  order  to  the
transferor as well as the transferee courts for taking
steps in accordance with this order.
Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.
………………………………………………………, J
(B.V. NAGARATHNA)

COPY OF IMPUGNED ORDER
 
Date : 22-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE B.V. NAGARATHNA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE UJJAL BHUYAN
For Petitioner(s) : Dr. A.M. Singhvi, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Gopal Sankaranarayanan, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Mahesh Agarwal, Adv.
                   Mr. Ayaz Bilawala, Adv.

                   Mr. Aayushman Agarwal, Adv.
                   Mr. E. C. Agrawala, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Permission to file Special Leave Petition is allowed.
We have heard Dr.A.M.Singhvi, learned senior counsel
for the petitioner.
1

--- Page 348 ---
One of the main contentions raised was with regard to
the  territorial  jurisdiction  of  the  Trial  Court  to
entertain the Title Suit No.137 of 2026.
It was submitted that the agreement was executed in
Mumbai and therefore, merely because the power of attorney
of the respondent-plaintiff resided in Kathiar would not
have  granted  the  Court  of  Additional  Judge,  Kathiar
jurisdiction to entertain the suit as there is also a
clause regarding jurisdiction in clause 9 of the agreement
dated 20.06.1997 conferring exclusive jurisdiction on the
courts at Mumbai. 
It was further submitted that the impugned order of
the Trial Court which is a subject matter of revision
before the High Court is coming in the way of the release
of the film ‘Hai Jawani Toh Ishq Hona Hai’, song and
trailer. That although the High Court has granted the next
date  as  15.05.2026,  it  was  submitted  that  in  the
interregnum prior to 05.06.2026, a number of contractual
arrangements would have to be made for the distribution of
the film which is all in jeopardy owing to the ex parte
impugned order passed by the Trial court.
Taking note of the said submissions, we issue notice
to the respondents.
Interim stay of the impugned  ex parte  order dated
06.05.2026 in Title Suit No.137 of 2026 pending on the file
of the Court of Additional Judge-I, Kathiar until the
disposal of the Civil Miscellaneous No.684 of 2026 by the
2

--- Page 349 ---
High Court of Judicature at Patna.
It is needless to observe that this order would not
come in the way of the High Court considering the matter on
merits on 25.05.2026.
(B. LAKSHMI MANIKYA VALLI)                      (DIVYA BABBAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) No. 401 of 2026
SUSHMITA SINGH … PETITIONER
VERSUS
SURAJ KUMAR SINGH … RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. The petitioner (wife) has filed this petition seeking
transfer of M.P. Case No. 873 of 2025 titled as Suraj Singh
vs. Sushmita Singh filed by the respondent (husband) under
Section 13(1) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 from the
Family Court at Deoria, Uttar Pradesh to the competent
Family Court at Tis Hazari Courts, Delhi.
2. Despite service of notice on the respondent, no one
has entered appearance on his behalf.  However, vide order
dated  23.04.2026,  awaiting  representation  of  the
respondent, the matter was adjourned.  Even today, there is
no one present on behalf of the respondent to oppose the
prayer as made in the transfer petition.
3. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner and
looking to the distance between Deoria, Uttar Pradesh and
Delhi, it would be inconvenient to the petitioner (wife) to
attend the proceedings at Deoria, Uttar Pradesh.

--- Page 357 ---
2
4. Therefore,  we  deem  it  appropriate  to  allow  this
transfer petition and accordingly,  M.P. Case No. 873 of
2025 titled as Suraj Singh vs. Sushmita Singh filed by the
respondent (husband) under Section 13(1) of the Hindu
Marriage Act, 1955 is ordered to be transferred from the
Family Court at Deoria, Uttar Pradesh to the competent
Family Court at Tis Hazari Courts, Delhi
5. It is on the discretion of the transferee Court to
allow the presence of the respondent (husband) virtually
until necessarily required.
6. On intimation by email, the records of the case be
transferred to the transferee Court without any delay and
immediate compliance be made. confirmed
7. The transfer petition is, accordingly, allowed.
8. Pending applications, if any, stand disposed of.
…………………………………………………,J.
[J.K. MAHESHWARI]
…………………………………………………,J.
[ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
New Delhi;
May 21, 2026.

1. This I.A. No. 16808 of 2026 has been filed by the respondent
no.7 and along with the said application he has attached the death
certificate issued by the Department of Public Health, Greater
Chennai Corporation. According to which N. Ramachandran (respondent
no.8) has passed away on 16.04.2026.
2. He further submits that the impugned order passed by the
Division Bench of the High Court was stayed by this Court vide
order dated 16.11.2015.
3. He further submits that the said order dated 16.11.2015 may be
made absolute and the petition may be disposed of as nothing
further survives to be done in this case.
4. The learned counsel for the respondent no.8 also admits to the
2

--- Page 367 ---
SLP(C)  No.31204/2015
fact about the death of his client.
5. Further Ms. Aishwarya Bhati, the learned ASG appearing for the
petitioner-Union of India, submits that a new policy is now in
place, enforced in 2024.  As such, any further decision for grant
of awards will be governed by the said policy.
6. In view of the above, we dispose of this petition, making the
interim order absolute.
7. Pending application(s), if any, stands disposed of.

ITEM NO.1               COURT NO.12               SECTION II-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  6062/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  03-01-2025
in CRM No. 26298/2023 passed by the High Court of Judicature at 
Patna]
GUDIA DEVI @ RAMESHWARI KUMARI                     Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF BIHAR & ANR.                              Respondent(s)
IA No. 92405/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 19-05-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Manoj Ranjan Sinha, Adv.
                   Mr. Vishal Agarwal, Adv.
                   Mr. Mrigank Prabhakar, AoR
                   Ms. Astha Singh, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Manish Kumar, AoR
                   Mr. Kumar Saurav, Adv.
                   Mr. Divyansh Mishra, Adv.
                   
                   Mr. Hitendra Nath Rath, AoR
    Mr. Chandan Prasad, Respondent 
No. 2
(Through Video Conferencing)
                   
                            O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioner.
2. At the outset, Mr. Hitendra Nath Rath, learned counsel for the
respondent No.2 submits that he may be permitted to withdraw from
the case as the respondent No.2 has expressed his desire to engage
another counsel. The respondent No. 2 has also joined the Court
proceedings through video conferencing.

--- Page 369 ---
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner draws the attention of this
Court to the order dated 26th February, 2026 in which the respondent
No.2 had undertaken to take the petitioner-wife to the matrimonial
home and also to give Rs. 2,00,000/- (Rupees Two Lakhs) by way of
Fixed Deposit Receipt in the name of the petitioner-wife, for the
present.
4. It is submitted that the admitted position is that the same
has not been complied with. The respondent No. 2 submits that the
he had gone to the house of the petitioner but the petitioner did
not come with him.
5. At this juncture, when the Court called upon learned counsel
for the petitioner to respond, he takes a specific stand in
presence of the petitioner, who is also present in Court, that on
the day though the respondent No.2 went to the house of the
petitioner to take her with him but he made it categorically clear
that the petitioner-wife would have to reside in the village home
at Nasriganj, Bihar whereas he would be living in Assam. Thus, for
the reason that the order had indicated that the respondent No. 2
would keep the petitioner-wife with him, the respondent No.2 being
not faithful and truthful to the said commitment, the petitioner-
wife was left with no option but to refuse going with him.
6. What  has  been  stated  by  the  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner-wife, if true, is sufficient ground for the petitioner-
wife not to have accompanied the respondent.
7. In the aforesaid background, in the tentative view of the
Court, the respondent No. 2 is clearly in contempt. Accordingly,
notice in contempt is issued against the respondent No.2. He shall

--- Page 370 ---
be physically present with his show-cause on 15.07.2026 when the
matter shall be listed at the top of the board.
8. In the meantime, it shall be open to the respondent No.2 to
engage a counsel of his choice.
9. Mr. Hitendra Nath Rath, learned AoR who till now was appearing
for the respondent No.2 is discharged.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (ANJALI PANWAR)
     COURT MASTER (SH)                       ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

--- Page 371 ---

For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Gaurav Agrawal, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Sravan Kumar Karanam, AOR
                   Mr. Kumar Abhishek, Adv.
                   Mr. P. Venkatraju, Adv.
                   Ms. Sushmitha M., Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :
Mr. Roshan Santhalia, AOR
Mr. Shivansh Sinha, Adv.
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1) Learned  counsel  for  the  petitioner  has.  on
instructions, supplied a letter dated 08.04.2026 issued by
the Director and COO of respondent No. 1.
1

--- Page 378 ---
2) We find that respondent No. 1 in the said letter has
intimated as under: 
“To
The Assistant Divisional Engineer,
Telangana State  Southern Power  Distribution Company
Limited (TGSPDCL),
Kothur Mandal, Rangareddy District, 
Telangana-509228
Subject: Request for Release of Electricity Connection
at New Premises
Respected Sir, 
We, Elifecycle Management Private Limited, had earlier
submitted an application for electricity connection to
our site located at Sy. No. 468, 470, 471 & 472, Chegur
Village, Kothur Mandal, Rangarddy District, Telangana –
509215.  However, the supply was not released owing to
the landlord’s outstanding dues payable to TGSPDCL.  We
would like to inform you that we no longer require
electricity connection at the above-mentioned site.
Further, we wish to inform you that we have shifted our
operations to a new premises situated at Sy. No. 8/A,
Kodicherla Village, Kothur Mandal, Rangareddy District,
Telangana – 509228.
In view of this relocation, we kindly request you to
take note of the change and release the electricity
connection  for  our  new  premises  at  the  earliest
convenience.  
We assure you of our compliance with all applicable
requirements  and  look  forward  to  your  support  in
enabling uninterrupted operations at our new facility.
Thanking you in anticipation. 
Yours faithfully,
For ELIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT PRIVATE LIMITED
Mohit Kumar Vanamala
Director & COO”
3) In this view of the matter, respondent No. 1 do not
require the electricity connection at the site and they wish
2

--- Page 379 ---
to shift the operations to a new premise situated at a
different  place  in  Kodicherla  Village,  Kothur  Mandal,
Rangareddy District, Telangana.  
4) We take the said letter on record.  
5) Leaving the question of law open, we dispose of this
special leave petition.  Pending application(s), if any,
shall stand disposed of.
(NIDHI AHUJA)                   (NAND KISHOR)
    DEPUTY REGISTRAR             ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. At the outset, we have been informed that the parties
have not arrived at a settlement in mediation.
1

--- Page 400 ---
SLP (C)  NO.  4414/2023
3. Having regard to the fact that the notice was issued
only with regard to the alimony and the matter was sent
for mediation on the said point and today the stand being
taken by the respondent is that he is not ready to pay
alimony and would rather pay monthly maintenance, the
Court finds the stand to be totally unreasonable and
unacceptable.  
4. Accordingly,  this  Court  was  of  the  view  that
interference was required in the matter.
5. At this juncture, learned counsel for the respondent
submits that one last opportunity be given to her to take
instructions from the respondent as to the quantum of
alimony he is ready to pay. We make it clear that the
amount has to be reasonable and just and not a token.
6. List on 27.07.2026.
7. Interim order passed by this Court on 16.02.2026 to
continue till the next date of listing.

At the outset, Mr. S.D. Sanjay, learned ASG appearing
on behalf of the respondent no.2-Union of India, submits
that as per the dictum of this Court in Criminal Appeal
No. 825 of 2026, titled “Zeba Khan Vs. State of U.P. and
Others” passed on 11.02.2026, antecedents of the accused
has mandatorily be disclosed in the synopsis itself, in
matters where bail petitions are filed. In the present
case, the same has not been done, the maintainability of
this petition itself is challenged. 
2.  Mr. V.N. Sinha, learned Senior Counsel for the
petitioner seeks time to respond.

ITEM NO.31               COURT NO.17               SECTION XVI
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) Diary No(s). 29919/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated  11-11-2025 in
CMJ No. 1407/2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at Patna]
M/S SADBHAV-GKC LTD. JV                            Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
BIHAR STATE ROAD DEVELOPMENT CORPORATION LTD       Respondent(s)
IA  No.  155700/2026  -  CONDONATION  OF  DELAY  IN  FILING,IA  No.
155701/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDL.DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 21-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Balbir Singh, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Akshat Agrawal, Adv.
                   Ms. Rita Jha, AOR
                   Ms. Disha Thakkar, Adv.
                   Mr. Raj Karn, Adv.
                   Mr. Mohit Agrawal, Adv.
                   Mr. Lokesh Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Rahul Kumar, Adv.
                   
For Respondent(s)  
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned. 
2. Heard Mr. Balbir Singh, learned Senior Counsel for the
petitioner. 
3. After the impugned order dated 11.11.2025, on 08.12.2025,
the petitioner has filed Interlocutory application for vacation
of the order before the High Court. Thereafter, an application
was filed to direct the Trial Judge to hear the petition under

--- Page 540 ---
2
Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 (for
short, 'A&C Act') filed by the respondent challenging the award
in favour of the petitioner. The High Court has directed that
the said application filed under Section 34 of the A&C Act
should be disposed of at the earliest. Learned Senior Counsel
for the petitioner submits that the award is of 17.03.2017 and
in spite of getting interim orders of attachment, today all the
orders remained stayed including the execution proceedings
itself.
4. Be that as it may, we are of the opinion that the
interest of justice would be served, if we direct the Court of
District Judge at Patna, Bihar to decide to Misc.Arbitration
Case No.36/2017 along with 80/2017 on or before 31.08.2026.
5. We are making this order, as the award is of 2017 and it
is already 09 years since the award has been passed.
6. With the above observations, the Special Leave Petition
is disposed of.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed

parties.
Petitioners’ suit for specific performance of the
agreement of sale has been dismissed for default and has
not yet been restored on the file of the Trial Court.

decree in respect of the subject house which is the subject

property to the respondent.

counsel for the respective parties sought for some time.

submits that there is no causa causans between the act of
the petitioner-doctor and the death of the deceased.

Date : 22-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Adhyayan Gupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Rohit Pandey, Adv.
                   Ms. Ashtha Shrivastav, Adv.

                   Mr. Aditya Kumar Tiwari, Adv.
                   Ms. Sadhana Sandhu, AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Adhyayan Gupta, learned counsel for the
petitioner.
2. Delay condoned.
1

--- Page 594 ---
3. Learned  counsel  submits  that  the  case  involves
commission of offence under Section 302 read with Section
34 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short, “the IPC”).
Though the Police initially filed the closure report, on
a Protest Petition, cognizance has been taken and summons
issued to the accused.  However, the High Court has,
while considering the petition to quash the proceedings
stayed the trial.  Learned counsel submits that stay is
operating for approximately 4 years.  
4. We are of the opinion that the High Court should
take  up  the  quashing  petition  and  dispose  it  of
expeditiously, preferably within a period of 3 months
from today.
5. A  copy  of  this  order  be  communicated  to  the
Registrar General of the Allahabad High Court.
6. The  Registrar  General  of  the  High  Court  upon
receipt of the order shall place the same before Hoin’ble
the Chief Justice of the Allahabad High Court.
7. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
8. Pending applications shall also stand disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)

1

--- Page 614 ---
3. Learned counsel contends that under Section 67(4)
of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (for
short, “the Act”) sealing of premises can be done only if
access is denied.  In the present case, according to the
learned  counsel,  Section  67(4)  of  the  Act  has  no
application.   Independently,  learned  counsel  submits
invoking Section 83 of the Act attachment of the bank
accounts have been made.  Learned counsel contends that
till date there is no Show Cause Notice issued.

THE STATE REP BY THE INSPECTOR OF POLICE           Respondent(s)

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. V N Subramaniam, Adv.
                   Mr. Hari Abhishek, Adv.                   
                   For M/S Vns Legal Partners, AOR                 
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. V.N.Subramaniam, learned counsel for the
petitioner.
2. At the outset, we must point out that an error has
crept into our order passed on 15 th May, 2026 when it
wrongly recorded that exemption from surrendering was
granted till the first date of listing by order of 6 th
April, 2026. In fact exemption from surrendering was not
1

--- Page 622 ---
granted on 6th  April, 2026 and all that happened was that
six  weeks'  time  was  granted  to  the  petitioner  to
surrender.  The petitioner has not surrendered and in
fact he is at large since 31st October, 2022 when the High
Court pronounced the judgment.
3. We direct the petitioner to surrender before the
concerned Court within two weeks from today.
4. Let necessary affidavit with proof of surrender be
filed in this Court.
5. List on 14th July, 2026.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Sanjay Singh, Adv.

                   Mr. Shalender Singh, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : Mrs. Anjana Prakash, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Anuj Prakaash, Adv.
                   Mr. Niraj Dubey, Adv.
                   Mr. Pradum Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Kumar Mihir, AOR                   
                   
                   Mrs. Rachna Gupta, AOR
                   Mr. Anil Kumar Sinha, Adv.
                   
                   Ms. Ruchira Goel, AOR          
     UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner challenges the impugned judgment and order
dated 29.01.2026 in Criminal Misc. Bail Application No.38361 of
2025, titled “Manish Mishra Vs. State of U.P. ”, passed by the
1

--- Page 704 ---
High Court of Judicature at Allahabad.
2. We have heard learned senior counsel/counsel(s) appearing
for the respective parties and perused the material available
on record. 
3. Even though we are not inclined to interfere with the
impugned judgment and order but however, we clarify that if the
private respondent is not cooperating in trial in any one  of
the cases referred to by the State in its counter affidavit, it
shall be open for the State/complainant to take steps for
cancellation of the bail. 
4. Liberty is also granted to the State to challenge the bail
orders in any of the other connected matters, wherein the
private respondent is an accused.
5. In the event that the trial is not progressing only on
account of non-appearance of the co-accused, the Trial Court
shall segregate the trial and ensure that trial, particularly
in respect of those cases which were registered prior to 2011,
is concluded expeditiously, and preferably within a period of
one year from today.
6. The learned counsel for the State undertakes that the
State shall produce all witnesses on such dates as may be fixed
by the Trial Court.
7. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition is disposed of.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
       (SOURAV PAL)                              (ANU BHALLA)
SENIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT                     COURT MASTER (NSH)

                            O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
2. Learned counsel for the petitioner has taken us to depositions
of various witnesses and also argued on the merits. The primary
contention is that there is discrepancy between the statement of
the witnesses relating to the presence of the petitioner and also
that two out of the six eye-witnesses have not even named the

--- Page 718 ---
petitioner.
3. Learned  counsel  for  the  State  submits  that  there  is
categorical assertion by four eye-witnesses with regard to the
presence as also overt act done by the petitioner. Moreover, it was
submitted that out of seventeen witnesses seven have been examined.
It is undertaken that the rest of the witnesses shall be positively
examined within the next four months. Let  the  same  be  done
positively.
4. Taking an overall view of the matter, we are not persuaded to
allow the prayer for bail to the petitioner, for the present.
5. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed.
6. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of. 
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (ANJALI PANWAR)

RAJKUMAR JHAMB                                     Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
PRATAP SINGH HADA                                  Respondent(s)
(FOR ADMISSION IA No. 158376/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 22-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.K. MAHESHWARI
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Akshat Gupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Pranav Jain, Adv.
                   Ms. Sakshi Tikmany, Adv.
                   Mr. Pranav Krishna, AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. After hearing learned counsel for the petitioner, we see no
reason and ground to entertain this special leave Petition.   
3. At this stage, learned counsel for the petitioner prays
for time to vacate the suit premises.  In the facts and
circumstances, we grant time to vacate the suit property up to
30.11.2026, subject to payment of arrears of rent within one
month and regular rent on month to month basis.  It is directed

--- Page 744 ---
2
that  the  petitioner  shall  hand  over  the  peaceful  vacant
possession of the premises to the respondent on or before
31.11.2026 and shall not part with or create third party right
therein.  A usual undertaking shall be filed within a period of
three weeks from today before the Registrar, High Court of
Judicature  for  Rajasthan  at  Jaipur.   Non-filing  of  the
undertaking shall give a cause to the landlord to execute the
decree and the time for vacation as specified above shall not
have any bearing to the execution proceedings.  Defiance of the
above terms, if any, may be treated as non-compliance of the
order of this Court.
4. This order has been passed without issuing notice to other
side, however, in case the respondent-landlord feels aggrieved
by the order granting time for vacation of the suit premises, he
is at liberty to apply before this Court.
5. The Special Leave Petition is, accordingly, dismissed with
above terms.  Pending applications, if any, shall stand disposed
of.
(GULSHAN KUMAR ARORA)                           (KOMAL)

in CRLP No. 4461/2026 passed by the High Court for The State of 
Telangana at Hyderabad]
HARISH KUMAR RATHI                                 Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF TELANGANA & ANR.                      Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
 
Date : 22-05-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Jayprakash Bansilal Somani, Adv.

                   Mr. Jeevan R. Patil, Adv.
                   Ms. Pooja Agarwal, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner seeks to impugn an order of
the  High  Court  for  the  State  of  Telangana  at
Hyderabad dated 26.03.2026 by which the High Court
1

--- Page 786 ---
accepted the prayer of the second respondent and
quashed the cognizance order on the ground that it
did not contain reasons.
2. As the High Court has left it open for the
learned  Magistrate  to  pass  a  fresh  order  in
accordance with law, we decline to entertain this
petition.
3. The special leave petition is, accordingly,
dismissed.
4. However, we deem it appropriate to observe
that  the  learned  Magistrate  shall  accord  due
consideration  to  the  police  report  and  pass
appropriate orders thereupon expeditiously and in
accordance with law.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

IA No. 151676/2026 - STAY APPLICATION

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Appellant(s) : Mr Dhruv Mehta, Sr. Adv.

                   Mr. Shivam Shorewala, Adv.
                   Ms. Rakshita Bhargava, Adv.
                   Ms. Archie Garg, Adv.
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The submission of the learned counsel for
the appellant is that by virtue of the Memorandum
of Agreement dated 27.04.2020, the appellant (M/s
Puneet India Pvt. Ltd.) was entitled to adjustment
1

--- Page 806 ---
of its dues payable by Belgotex South Africa from
the dues payable to Belgotex India Private Limited.
The Belgotex India Private Limited was also a party
to the said Memorandum of Agreement and the said
Memorandum  of  Agreement  was  produced  by  the
respondent.
2. It is the case of the appellant that if the
amount  payable  thereunder  is  taken  into
consideration, the amount due by the appellant to
the respondent would be below the threshold for
invocation of the provisions of the Insolvency and
Bankruptcy Code, 2016.
3. Issue notice, returnable in the first week
of August, 2026.
4. Notice on behalf of the respondent has been
accepted by Mr. Chitranshul A. Sinha, AOR.
5. Till the next date of listing, the effect
and operation of the impugned order shall remain
stayed.

--- Page 840 ---
For Petitioner(s) :  Petitioner-in-person (N.P.)
                    
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Neelmani Pant, AoR
                   
                            O R D E R
The  petitioner  was  appearing  in  person  on  the  previous
occasion(s). Today, neither the petitioner is present nor anybody
is appearing on his behalf when the matter is called out
2. Learned counsel for the respondent draws our attention to our
earlier orders dated 27th October, 2025 and 15th May, 2026. From the
same, it is clear that direction was given to the petitioner to
comply with and act on the stand taken by the petitioner before the
Court with regard to paying a certain amount to the respondent,
whereupon the respondent undertook to withdraw the complaint filed
by the respondent, but the petitioner had not paid any amount so
far.
3. In the aforesaid background, we find that the Court is now
required to pass appropriate orders going by the conduct of the
petitioner, but we refrain from doing so at the present and we give
one  last  opportunity  to  the  petitioner  to  comply  with  the
directions.
4. List the matter on 28.05.2026.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (ANJALI PANWAR)
     COURT MASTER (SH)                       ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

                            O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioner.
2. Delay in filing the amended cause title is condoned.
3. At the outset, it has been pointed out by learned counsel for
the petitioner that two of the respondents have not been served.
Upon verification, it transpires that the service report indicates
that there are no such persons at the address supplied by the
petitioner. 
4. In view of the aforesaid, learned counsel for the petitioner
submits that she will verify the names as well as the correct
present address of the two respondents and take appropriate steps.
Let the same be done within three weeks from today.
5. Office is directed to send notice on the fresh and correct
address thereafter.
4. List on 27.08.2026.

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  29-04-2026
in WPL No. 8840/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Bombay]
JAVED AZIZ KHAN & ORS.                             Petitioner(s)

THE UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                          Respondent(s)
(IA No. 156378/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 156376/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
IA No. 156373/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE PETITION (SLP/TP/WP/..)
 
Date : 19-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE B.V. NAGARATHNA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE UJJAL BHUYAN
For Petitioner(s)  Ms. Nina R Nariman, Adv.
                   Mr. Moinuddin Chaudhary, Adv.
                   Ms. Suchi A Waghny, Adv.
                   Mr. Aswani Saini, Adv.
                   Mr. Svarit Uniyal Mishra, Adv.
                   Ms. Padmja Mishra, Adv.
                   Mr. Danish Saifi, Adv.
                   Mr. Vv Kk Hanumanth Prasad, Adv.
                   Mr. Rashid N. Azam, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Application for permission to file the petition is
allowed.
2. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioners.
3. We dispose of this Special Leave Petition by observing
that the petitioners can make a representation for
implementation of the directions issued by this Court
in the case of  Utran Se Besthan Railway Jhopadpatti

--- Page 874 ---
Vikas Mandal vs. Government of India and Ors. in SLP(C)
Diary No.19714/2021 dated 16.12.2021 insofar as the
direction Nos.6, 7 and 8 are concerned which are
extracted as under:
“(vi) In the event, the local Government has any
rehabilitation scheme, the affected persons may apply
for being rehabilitated under the said scheme, if
eligible and subject to verification of eligibility and
complying with all other terms and conditions of the
prevalent scheme. The local Government may provide them
suitable  residential  accommodation  in  lieu  of
rehabilitation owing to demolition of their structure. 
(vii) If no rehabilitation scheme has been formulated
by the local Government or is in force, the persons
likely to be affected by the action of demolition can
apply for allocation of residential premises under the
Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojna Scheme, which application be
processed not later than six months from the date of
its receipt and taken to its logical end, application-
wise within such period.
(viii) Be it a case of rehabilitation under clause
(vi) or (vii) above, the persons affected by demolition
action by the Authorities cannot insist for allotment
of alternative residential accommodation at the same
place from where they have been evicted (as it is not
in situ rehabilitation programme). The eligible persons
be allotted accommodation wherever available in the
same or even in neighbouring districts.”
4. It is needless to observe that if any application is
made by the petitioners herein for relief in accordance
with the aforesaid directions issued by this Court, the
same shall be considered as expeditiously as possible
and in accordance with law.
5. We however make it clear that we have not interfered
with the impugned order.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
(RADHA SHARMA)                                  (DIVYA BABBAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

Date : 22-05-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARAVIND KUMAR
         HON'BLE  MR. JUSTICE PRASANNA B. VARALE
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. C U Singh, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Pallavi Pratap, AOR
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The prayer sought for in the present petition is similar and
identical to the prayer that was sought in W.P. (C) No.582/2026
which  has  been  disposed  of  by  this  Court  vide  order  dated
11.05.2026 whereunder it was observed as follows:
“5. Mr.  C  U  Singh,  learned  senior  counsel
appearing  for  some  of  the  petitioners  in  the
unlisted matter(s) would also hasten to add that
some  of  the  Advocates  have  already  submitted
representation(s) to the Hon’ble Chief Justice of
India in this regard and pray for some being
considered favourably.
6. Having  regard  to  the  totality  of
circumstances of the case, we are of the considered
view that ends of justice would be met by directing
the  petitioners  to  submit  a  comprehensive  and
exhaustive  representation  to  the  Hon’ble  Chief
Justice of India and in the event of such a
1

--- Page 890 ---
representation being filed, we see no reason as to
why the Hon’ble Chief Justice of India would not
consider  it  sympathetically  by  taking  into
consideration  all  the  circumstances  prevalent
including  the  grievances  addressed  in  the
respective representations that are being filed or
already filed. We request the Hon’ble Chief Justice
of India to consider the said representation on
administrative side.” 
2. In view of the same, the order that would be passed by the
Hon’ble Chief Justice of India by considering the representation
would also govern the petitioners in the instant case. Hence, we
are not inclined to entertain the present petition. With this
observation, Writ Petition stands disposed of.
3. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
  (NEHA GUPTA)                                   (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1) Assailing  judgment  dated  22.11.2021  passed  by  the
Madras High Court in C.M.S.A. No. 5 of 2019 granting decree
of divorce against the wife(petitioner herein), the present
petition has been filed. 
1

--- Page 916 ---
SLP (C) No. 14852/2022
2) Vide order dated 25.08.2025, the mater was referred to
the mediation.  The mediated settlement dated 15.11.2025 has
been received from the Supreme Court Mediation Centre vide
communication dated 20.11.2025 .  As per the settlement, a
sum of Rs.17,00,000/- (Rupees Seventeen Lakhs) has been paid
to the wife and Rs.20,000/-(Rupees Twenty Thousand) towards
the  premium  amount  of  the  insurance  policy  of  the
son(Dhanesh Chandran) has been paid.  In terms thereto, it
was decided that all the proceedings pending between the
parties shall be withdrawn and be treated as disposed of.  
3) Considering the aforesaid, maintaining the decree of
divorce in terms of the mediated settlement which shall be a
part of this order and the decree, in full satisfaction of
the  parties,  the  present  special  leave  petition  stands
disposed of.  Cases, if any, pending between the parties
shall  be  treated  as  disposed  of  and  consigned  to  the
records. 
4) Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
(NIDHI AHUJA)                   (NAND KISHOR)

AVINASH KUMAR & ORS.                               Respondent(s)
(IA No. 153651/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING and IA No.
149840/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING and IA No. 121202/2026
- EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T. and IA No. 132416/2026 - PERMISSION TO
FILE PETITION (SLP/TP/WP/..))
 
Date : 19-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.K. MAHESHWARI
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s) : 
Mr. Sanjeev Sahay, Adv.
                   Mr. Sameer Ranjan, Adv.
                   Mr. Vatsalya Vigya, AOR
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1) Permission to file contempt petition is granted. 
2) Delay condoned.
3) Mr. Sanjeev Sahay, learned counsel appearing for the
petitioners,  submits  that  in  view  of  the  directions
contained in judgment dated 30.10.2025 in Parimal Kumar and
Others v. The State of Jharkhand and Others (Civil Appeal
No. 1297 of 2025), in particular, para 63 (i), (ii) and
(iii)  thereof,  the  authorities  cannot  issue  show  cause
1

--- Page 922 ---
CONMT.PET.(C) No. 282/2026 in C.A. No. 1297/2025
notices asking the completion of the course subject wise.
4) In our view, the said issue has not been answered in
the judgment (supra), more so, no direction has been issued
in that regard.  Therefore, the petitioners are at liberty
to reply the notice and in case any adverse order is passed
against them, they are free to raise the issue before the
High Court.
5) With the aforesaid observations, the present contempt
petition stands disposed of.
(NIDHI AHUJA)                   (NAND KISHOR)
    DEPUTY REGISTRAR               ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
2

Date : 19-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE M.M. SUNDRESH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Siddharth Aggarwal, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Malak Manish Bhatt, AOR
                   Mr. Varun Byreddy, Adv.
                   Mr. Saahil Bahety, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Application  for  exemption  from  filing  official
translation is allowed.   
Learned senior counsel appearing for the petitioners
submits that we are dealing with the second FIR. On the very same
allegation, an earlier FIR was registered, which has been closed. 
Though the private respondent has stated that she filed a
protest petition, she has chosen to file a fresh FIR which is an
abuse of the process of law.
Issue notice, returnable on 13.07.2026.
In the meantime, further proceedings arising out of FIR
No.104/2022, registered at P.S. Vijayawada shall remain stayed.

filing application for restoration is condoned.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  17585/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  29-04-2026
in WP(C) No. 5614/2026 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New
Delhi]
M/S LUNAR CONSULTANTS AND DEVELOPERS               Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
DELHI DEVELOPMENT AUTHORITY & ORS.                 Respondent(s)
IA No. 151840/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT, IA No. 151841/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 22-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ALOK ARADHE
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Bhadresh Harikant, Adv.
Mr. Vaibhav Dwivedi, Adv. 
Mr. Shivam Tiwari, Adv. 
Mr. A. Selvin Raja, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
   UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned counsel for the petitioner seeks leave to withdraw
this Special Leave Petition to enable him to file Review Petition
before the High Court.    
2. Permission is granted.

Court, under the rules.
